greeting: Hey! I'm Kairu. CSEC English A rewards careful reading. Vocabulary, grammar, error-spotting and comprehension all reward students who read every option before picking. If two options seem right, look for the BEST — usually the one most directly supported by the passage. Cross-check any tricky comprehension calls with your teacher.

questions.stem: Despite his short-temper, his willingness to give of his wealth to help others to succeed led people to call him ____.
questions.explanation: 'Benevolent' means generous and well-meaning, fitting someone who shares his wealth to help others despite his temper.

distractors.A: Relates to short-temper, not to generosity.
distractors.B: Negative and unrelated to giving wealth.
distractors.D: No connection to generosity.

questions.stem: When parents display a ____ for one child, the others are likely to feel ____.
questions.explanation: Showing a 'preference' for one child naturally causes the others to feel 'jealous'. This pair best fits the cause-effect logic.

distractors.A: Fondness for one does not specifically cause others to feel lonely.
distractors.B: Dislike for one would not make others feel indifferent.
distractors.D: Concern for one would not normally make others angry.

questions.stem: An attack on someone when that person is most vulnerable may be ____ but is ____.
questions.explanation: The connector 'but' signals contrast. 'Effective' (positive in tactical terms) contrasts with 'unkind' (morally negative), making this pair the most logical contrasting completion.

distractors.A: Inappropriate and thoughtful do not contrast meaningfully.
distractors.B: Both rewarding and satisfying are positive, no contrast.
distractors.C: Both destructive and distressing are negative, no contrast.

questions.stem: While the committee members ____ insults across the floor, the chairman tried in vain to ____ order.
questions.explanation: Insults are 'hurled' (an idiomatic collocation) and order, once lost, is 'restored'. The pair fits both the violent imagery and the logic of trying to bring order back.

distractors.C: 'Gave insults' is awkward and 'maintain' implies order already existed.
distractors.D: 'Conserve order' is not idiomatic.

questions.stem: To conceal his true activities, the spy quickly ____ a plausible excuse for his presence there.
questions.explanation: To 'fabricate' is to invent or make up, exactly what a spy concealing his activities would do with a plausible excuse.

distractors.B: Divulged means revealed, the opposite of concealing.
distractors.C: Explained is too neutral and would not conceal.
distractors.D: One does not disguise an excuse; one disguises oneself.

questions.stem: Choose the word most nearly OPPOSITE in meaning to the underlined word: He was EAGER to share the news with his friends.
questions.explanation: 'Eager' means keen and willing; 'reluctant' means unwilling, a direct opposite.

distractors.D: Refers to absence, not lack of willingness.

questions.stem: Choose the word most nearly OPPOSITE in meaning to the underlined word: Many parents and even some teenagers do not approve of the amount of PERMISSIVENESS in today's society.
questions.explanation: 'Permissiveness' means allowing freedom; its opposite is 'restrictiveness', meaning imposing limits.

distractors.D: Closer to a synonym than an antonym.

questions.stem: Choose the word most nearly OPPOSITE in meaning to the underlined word: Every effort was made to AVERT the chance of failure.
questions.explanation: To 'avert' means to prevent or ward off. 'Ensure' (to make certain something happens) is its direct opposite.

distractors.B: Close in meaning to avert, not opposite.
distractors.D: Means to defeat after encountering, not the opposite of preventing.

questions.stem: Choose the word most nearly OPPOSITE in meaning to the underlined word: The president of the students' association never mentioned the treasurer's INTEGRITY.
questions.explanation: 'Integrity' means honesty and uprightness; its direct opposite is 'dishonesty'.

questions.stem: Choose the phrase most nearly OPPOSITE in meaning to the underlined words: She seems to be SENSITIVE TO the feelings of others.
questions.explanation: To be 'sensitive to' feelings means to be aware of and responsive to them. The direct opposite is to be 'unaware of' those feelings.

distractors.D: Addresses opinion-forming, not sensitivity.

questions.stem: Revise the sentence so it begins with 'It is impossible for'. Original: 'All people from crowded nations cannot get over the accessibility of Canada's natural environment, in all its magnificence.' Choose the phrase that best continues 'It is impossible for ... all people from crowded nations ...':
questions.explanation: After 'It is impossible for [someone]', an infinitive is required. 'To get over' preserves the original idiom and meaning.

distractors.A: Loses the idiomatic sense of 'get over'.
distractors.D: Ungrammatical; an infinitive cannot take '-ing'.

questions.stem: Revise the sentence so it begins with 'Were Charlie to invite me'. Original: 'If Charlie invites me, I will go to his party.'
questions.explanation: 'Were Charlie to invite me' is a hypothetical (subjunctive) opening that pairs with 'I would go' to maintain the conditional structure.

distractors.D: Incomplete and past unreal.

questions.stem: Revise so the sentence begins with 'A learned man'. Original: 'More is experienced in one day of the life of a learned man than an ignorant man experiences in his whole lifetime.'

options.D: has more experience in one day

questions.explanation: The original uses simple present ('is experienced ... experiences'). 'A learned man experiences more in one day' maintains the present-tense parallel with 'than an ignorant man experiences'.

distractors.B: Makes 'more experienced' a comparative adjective, changing meaning.
distractors.D: Shifts from action to possession; less parallel.

questions.stem: Revise so the sentence begins with 'Having made'. Original: 'She found, even after she had made several attempts to regulate the sprinkler, that the water was still reaching and wetting the furniture on the verandah.'
questions.explanation: 'Having made several attempts to regulate the sprinkler, she still found that the water was reaching the furniture' is the correct participial construction. 'Still' captures the persistence implied by 'even after'.

distractors.B: Past perfect clashes with the participial opener.
distractors.D: Produces a fragment without a finite main verb.

questions.stem: Revise so the sentence begins with 'Many of the spectators stormed'. Original: 'The cricket match ended abruptly when many of the spectators stormed the field.' Choose the word that best completes 'Many of the spectators stormed the field, ____ the cricket match (to end) abruptly.'
questions.explanation: A present participle 'ending' creates a logical cause-and-effect link: the spectators stormed the field, thereby ending the cricket match abruptly.

distractors.C: Does not idiomatically combine with 'the cricket match'.
distractors.D: Means the match did not end, opposite of the original.

questions.stem: Identify the inappropriate underlined part (or D if none). 'Considering the chaos you would cause if you were to resign now, (A) I have (B) no choice as (C) to refuse your request.'

options.A: Considering the chaos you would cause

questions.explanation: The correct idiom is 'no choice but to', not 'no choice as to'. Option C contains the error.

distractors.D: There is an error, so D is wrong.

questions.stem: Identify the inappropriate underlined part (or D if none). 'You may notice that (A) the windows of the house next to the playground (B) looks like those of (C) ancient English cottages.'

options.A: the windows of the house next to the playground

questions.explanation: The subject is plural ('windows'), so the verb should be 'look', not 'looks'. Option B contains the subject-verb agreement error.

distractors.D: There is an error, so D is wrong.

questions.stem: Identify the inappropriate underlined part (or D if none). '(A) There is hardly a point to (B) our knocking as (C) no one is not at home.'

options.A: There is hardly a point to
options.C: no one is not at home

questions.explanation: 'No one is not at home' is a double negative. It should read 'no one is at home'. Option C contains the error.

distractors.D: There is an error, so D is wrong.

questions.stem: Identify the inappropriate underlined part (or D if none). '(A) Tom, the most talented of those musicians who need help, has (B) promised to give his services (C) free of charge for any worthwhile cause.'

options.A: Tom, the most talented of those musicians who need help, has

questions.explanation: All three underlined parts are grammatically and idiomatically correct. The sentence is acceptable as it stands.

distractors.A: Subject-verb agreement and superlative are correct.

questions.stem: Identify the inappropriate underlined part (or D if none). 'Any disease of the (A) bones and joints (B) which is due to deficiencies in (C) a single nutritional factor can be prevented.'
questions.explanation: The error lies in this clause's verb form/preposition.

distractors.D: There is an error, so D is wrong.

questions.stem: Choose the option that best describes the sentence: 'There are many who have not considered the need to abstain from alcohol, but sobriety is truly the only option for one who wishes to engage in healthy lifestyle practices.'

options.A: The sentence is wordy, is repetitive or contains redundancies.
options.B: The sentence contains a cliche or misused metaphors.
options.C: The sentence is incorrect grammatically or faulty in diction.
options.D: The sentence is acceptable as it stands.

questions.explanation: 'Abstain from alcohol' and 'sobriety' communicate the same idea twice, making the sentence wordy/redundant.

distractors.B: No obvious cliche or misused metaphor.
distractors.D: Not acceptable because of the redundancy.

questions.stem: Choose the option that best describes the sentence: 'He wanted to beg for his old job, but that ship has sailed, and he had to simply play the hand that was dealt him.'

options.A: The sentence is wordy, is repetitive or contains redundancies.
options.B: The sentence contains a cliche or misused metaphors.
options.C: The sentence is incorrect grammatically or faulty in diction.
options.D: The sentence is acceptable as it stands.

questions.explanation: The sentence strings together two cliches ('that ship has sailed' and 'play the hand that was dealt him').

distractors.D: Cliches make it unacceptable.

questions.stem: Choose the option that best describes the sentence: 'Rising from the throne, the crowd cheered their noble monarch as he walked solemnly forward to greet the foreign dignitaries.'

options.A: The sentence is wordy, is repetitive or contains redundancies.
options.B: The sentence contains a cliche or misused metaphors.
options.C: The sentence is incorrect grammatically or faulty in diction.
options.D: The sentence is acceptable as it stands.

questions.explanation: 'Rising from the throne' is a dangling/misplaced modifier; it incorrectly modifies 'the crowd' rather than the monarch. This is a grammatical/diction fault.

distractors.D: Dangling modifier makes it unacceptable.

questions.stem: Choose the option that best describes the sentence: 'The moon shone brightly in the clear night sky, its cool light a blessing after the searing heat of day.'

options.A: The sentence is wordy, is repetitive or contains redundancies.
options.B: The sentence contains a cliche or misused metaphors.
options.C: The sentence is incorrect grammatically or faulty in diction.
options.D: The sentence is acceptable as it stands.

questions.explanation: The sentence is grammatically correct, vivid and free of cliches. It is acceptable as it stands.

questions.stem: Choose the option that best describes the sentence: 'When the clerk raised his voice in protest, his boss asked him to hold his tongue since empty vessels made the most noise and are often thrown in the den to be eaten by the lions.'

options.A: The sentence is wordy, is repetitive or contains redundancies.
options.B: The sentence contains a cliche or misused metaphors.
options.C: The sentence is incorrect grammatically or faulty in diction.
options.D: The sentence is acceptable as it stands.

questions.explanation: The sentence mixes 'empty vessels make the most noise' with the biblical 'thrown to the lions', clichés and mixed metaphors.

distractors.D: Mixed metaphors make it unacceptable.

questions.stem:
The phrase "Her garden looks" (line 1) is an example of

Her garden looks in through my window
Criss-crossed by the white lattice.
Coolers they call them but they are also
Hiding places for small girls playing.
Her garden stands neatly round her house
Travels politely unto the verandah
To sit in pots or hang
Leafily down from large, earth-coloured urns.

She lives with Mama, shepherding her with
her full body
The hesitant ins and outs of Mama's half-blind days.
Feeding her frail consciousness with edited
Gleaner news
And home-made chicken soup.

In her home, borrowed children touched her
china birds with hands
Wiped clean from eating sticky cakes, each
with a cherry on top.
Or press moist, breathless kisses round
The corners of her smile.

Sometimes she fills the space out
With music. Spreading out nostalgia through
Strings and flutes, old-fashioned love songs
Of blue moons and forever until.

Shameful peeping Tom, I sit silent in
My lattice watching the even shape of her days
To catch, just once, a wider open door behind
Her steady eyes.

But in her green-edged privacy, self-contained
She keeps the half-drawn shutters of her life
Open just so, and mocks my greed and restlessness
With a calm refusal to be other than she
seems.

Poem: "An Even Shape"
questions.explanation: Giving the garden the human ability to 'look' is personification.

distractors.B: A metaphor would directly equate the garden with something else.
distractors.C: There is no mocking irony here.

options.A: poet was looking at it through a lattice window
options.B: garden beds were laid out in squares
options.C: children were walking across the garden
options.D: person looking at the garden was cross-eyed

questions.explanation: The lattice window the speaker looks through creates a criss-cross pattern over the garden view.

distractors.B: The poem says nothing about square garden beds.
distractors.C: The garden is being watched, not crossed by children.
distractors.D: There is no reference to anyone being cross-eyed.

questions.stem:
Which of the following phrases is meant to be taken literally?

Her garden looks in through my window
Criss-crossed by the white lattice.
Coolers they call them but they are also
Hiding places for small girls playing.

Travels politely unto the verandah

her full body

Poem: "An Even Shape"

options.B: 'Her garden ... / Travels politely unto the verandah' (lines 5–6)
options.D: '... watching the even shape of her days' (lines 28–29)

questions.explanation: Children really do hide behind lattice 'coolers' — this phrase is meant literally. The others use figurative language.

distractors.B: A garden cannot literally travel — this is personification.
distractors.C: Consciousness cannot literally be fed — metaphor.
distractors.D: Days cannot literally have an 'even shape'.

options.A: there were several well-kept potted plants on stands around her house
options.B: several tall trees stood along the side of the house
options.C: the garden which surrounded the house was well kept

distractors.A: Misreads 'stands' as the noun for plant stands.
distractors.D: 'Stands neatly' describes the garden, not cleaning.

distractors.D: 'Consciousness' refers to the mind.

distractors.C: Wiping hands is also an act of love.
distractors.D: Eating cakes is not described as an act of love.

questions.stem:
Which word BEST describes the homeowner's attitude towards the hillside near to his house?

Marcus wheeled himself out to his accustomed place at the table on the terrace and the sight struck him immediately. How could it not? It was an abomination, a desecration, a heresy, a sight unbelievable. There was a house on the hillside! His hill, the one which overlooked the village and which his terrace faced. It was a small house to be sure; he estimated it to be no more than twenty feet by twelve, with one door and one window on the long side which faced him. From the distance, it was no more than a doll's house, or what a child might draw; a wooden hut similar to those which sprung up daily in the squatter settlements which everywhere littered the hillsides, ringing the city, visible evidence of society out of control. But his hillside wasn't a squatter settlement and no other house marred his view. His house sat on a slight rise above the village in the valley, and from there he had an uninterrupted view of the mountains on all sides. Far away there were houses, proper ones, that is; but none close enough to mar his vision.

It had never occurred to him that the hillside he gazed at every day would ever be defaced with human habitation. It was so steep and inhospitable. Few trees grew on it except for the occasional clump of mangoes, it was mostly covered with grass which burned every year in the dry months and agaves which from time to time flowered yellow. Half way up the hill and directly in front of his favourite seat on the terrace was the only tree of any size and this was a silk-cotton tree which would one day become a giant but which was still small as cotton trees go. Year after year he watched its progress as it shed its leaves in the winter months and turned a bright green in the spring. And now immediately behind this tree, as if sheltering in its arms, someone had built a house. On his hill. It wasn't really his hill, but who else was there to claim it, who else had gazed at it three hundred and sixty-five mornings every year for the past twelve years?

Extract: "The View from the Terrace"
questions.explanation: Marcus repeatedly calls it 'his hill' — he treats the hillside as personal property even though it isn't his. That is possessiveness.

distractors.A: Anger comes after the new house appears; the underlying attitude is possessive.
distractors.C: He's deeply unhappy about the new house.

questions.stem:
The phrase "sat on" (line 9) tells us that the house is

Marcus wheeled himself out to his accustomed place at the table on the terrace and the sight struck him immediately. How could it not? It was an abomination, a desecration, a heresy, a sight unbelievable. There was a house on the hillside! His hill, the one which overlooked the village and which his terrace faced. It was a small house to be sure; he estimated it to be no more than twenty feet by twelve, with one door and one window on the long side which faced him. From the distance, it was no more than a doll's house, or what a child might draw; a wooden hut similar to those which sprung up daily in the squatter settlements which everywhere littered the hillsides, ringing the city, visible evidence of society out of control. But his hillside wasn't a squatter settlement and no other house marred his view. His house sat on a slight rise above the village in the valley, and from there he had an uninterrupted view of the mountains on all sides. Far away there were houses, proper ones, that is; but none close enough to mar his vision.

It had never occurred to him that the hillside he gazed at every day would ever be defaced with human habitation. It was so steep and inhospitable. Few trees grew on it except for the occasional clump of mangoes, it was mostly covered with grass which burned every year in the dry months and agaves which from time to time flowered yellow. Half way up the hill and directly in front of his favourite seat on the terrace was the only tree of any size and this was a silk-cotton tree which would one day become a giant but which was still small as cotton trees go. Year after year he watched its progress as it shed its leaves in the winter months and turned a bright green in the spring. And now immediately behind this tree, as if sheltering in its arms, someone had built a house. On his hill. It wasn't really his hill, but who else was there to claim it, who else had gazed at it three hundred and sixty-five mornings every year for the past twelve years?

Extract: "The View from the Terrace"
questions.explanation: 'Sat on' suggests the house rests on top of the slope rather than being built into it.

distractors.B: The tone is hostile, not comfortable.

questions.stem:
The writer's MAIN purpose in the extract is to

To the prejudiced eyes of land-bound humans the oceans seem like one continuous mass as homogeneous as outer space. To some extent they are, and some marine creatures treat the whole maritime world as their oyster. Some of the great whales, for instance, lunge from the surface to the depths as a matter of course, and divide their feeding and breeding between the poles and the tropics.

Equally striking to the marine scientist, however, is the variousness of the oceans. Each sea embraces several or indeed many distinct environments, each of which occupies a discrete zone. Some of these zones also vary markedly with time — through the day; with the tides, and therefore with the phases of the moon; by season; and sometimes in cycles of several years. And superimposed on all these variations in space and time are the more erratic influences of currents and of the influx of rivers.

In short, patchiness, in space and time, is as much a feature of the oceans as it is of land; indeed, 'patchiness' is a great principle in ecology — though it is rarely singled out as such. Each oceanic zone has its characteristic creatures — sometimes a huge variety of different types, and sometimes only a few; but many creatures, of all kinds, spend part of their lives in one kind of environment, and part in another. And because there are so many different ways of making a living in the oceans — so many permutations of habitats — there is a correspondingly huge variety of creatures; and many creatures take quite different forms, and live in quite different ways, at different stages of their lives. There are far fewer species in the oceans than on land, however, because there are no marine equivalents of the forest trees; and it is the trees that provide such a myriad of habitats for land-based creatures.

Extract: "The Oceans" by Colin Tudge, The Museum of Natural History, 1991.

options.A: show that the ocean is very diverse
options.B: convince the reader to visit the ocean
options.C: describe the beauty of the ocean to humans
options.D: discuss the different creatures that live in the ocean

questions.explanation: Tudge's central argument is that the oceans only appear uniform — in fact each sea contains many distinct environments, zones and varieties of creatures.

distractors.C: Beauty is not the focus; variety is.
distractors.D: Creatures are examples, not the main topic.

questions.stem:
The advertisement was most likely taken from a

The tours we offer are truly unique and very much off the beaten track. We take you into the country to experience the historical, cultural and breathtaking scenic highlights of the island. Our rich heritage in farming over the centuries has produced grand plantation mansions surrounded by picturesque little copses with stately royal palms swaying majestically in the easterly tradewinds.

The neat sugar cane fields sweep down the rolling hills towards the wild east coast where miles of untouched beaches are graced with the backdrop of rugged cliffs and the giant breakers of the Atlantic crashing ashore.

At Highland Outdoor Tours the choice is yours — whether you're the adventurous type or prefer a more laid-back tour, just give us a call and let us advise you.

questions.stem:
Which of the following is nearest in meaning to "off the beaten track" (line 1)?

The tours we offer are truly unique and very much off the beaten track. We take you into the country to experience the historical, cultural and breathtaking scenic highlights of the island. Our rich heritage in farming over the centuries has produced grand plantation mansions surrounded by picturesque little copses with stately royal palms swaying majestically in the easterly tradewinds.

The neat sugar cane fields sweep down the rolling hills towards the wild east coast where miles of untouched beaches are graced with the backdrop of rugged cliffs and the giant breakers of the Atlantic crashing ashore.

questions.stem:
What does the airline promise if a person joins the Miles Programme?

JOIN OUR CARIBBEAN MILES PROGRAMME

You can earn Caribbean Airlines Miles every flight; plus when you fly, drive or shop, you will earn miles even faster.

OUR MEMBERSHIP:
• Members who fly 20 000 miles on Caribbean Airlines within a calendar year are eligible for a Silver Membership.
• Flying 40 000 miles qualifies members for a Gold Membership, while flying 80 000 miles qualifies members for an Executive Gold Membership.

SILVER TIER MEMBERSHIP
• Unique member credentials
• A 3 000 Miles Bonus upon attainment of Silver Tier Status
• Preferential check-in and priority boarding on all Caribbean Airlines flights
• Waiver of date change penalty and redeposit fee on reward tickets

GOLD TIER MEMBERSHIP
• Unique member credentials
• A 5 000 Miles Bonus upon attainment of Gold Tier Status
• Preferential check-in and priority boarding on all Caribbean Airlines flights
• Waiver of date change penalty and redeposit fee on reward tickets
• Expedited award delivery at no cost

DESTINATION / AWARDS / MILES NEEDED
Within the Caribbean or between South America* and the Caribbean:
One Way Upgrade — 5 000
One Way Economy Class — 7 500
One Way Business Class — 12 000
Between the Caribbean / South America* and North America:
One Way Upgrade — 10 000
One Way Economy Class — 15 500
One Way Business Class — 25 500
Between Kingston and North America:
One Way Economy Class — 16 250
One Way Business Class — 28 750

* South America refers to Caracas, Georgetown & Paramaribo.

Thank you for flying with Caribbean Airlines.
Visit us at caribbean-airlines.com or call us.
E-mail: miles@caribbean-airlines.com

Advertisement adapted from "Caribbean Miles", Caribbean Beat, No. 108, March/April 2011, Media and Education Projects Ltd, p. 91.

distractors.C: Flying more is the means, not the goal.

questions.stem: Choose the word or phrase which is closest to OPPOSITE in meaning to the underlined word: We found her <u>appreciative of</u> the help that we had given her on this matter.
questions.explanation: 'Appreciative of' means grateful for, so its opposite is 'ungrateful for'. The other choices express displeasure but not the direct lack of gratitude that 'appreciative' implies.

distractors.A: 'Unhappy about' indicates dissatisfaction but not the lack of gratitude that 'appreciative' implies.
distractors.B: 'Displeased at' suggests annoyance, not the opposite of being grateful.
distractors.D: 'Intolerant of' means unwilling to accept, which is not the direct opposite of 'appreciative'.

questions.stem: Choose the word or phrase which is closest to OPPOSITE in meaning to the underlined word: Their actions showed that they were firm in their <u>rejection</u> of the new law.
questions.explanation: 'Rejection' means refusal to accept; its opposite is 'acceptance'.

distractors.A: 'Ignorance' relates to lack of knowledge, not to refusing/accepting.
distractors.B: 'Application' refers to putting something into use, not the opposite of rejection.
distractors.D: 'Understanding' relates to comprehension, not approval.

questions.stem: Choose the word or phrase which is closest to OPPOSITE in meaning to the underlined word: <u>Constructive</u> criticism is neither appreciated nor encouraged by some of our peers.
questions.explanation: 'Constructive' means helpful and positive; the direct opposite is 'negative'.

distractors.A: 'Sound' is a synonym (reliable), not an antonym.
distractors.C: 'Valuable' is a synonym suggesting worth, not the opposite.
distractors.D: 'Unsatisfactory' refers to quality rather than the opposite of constructive (which is negative/destructive).

questions.stem: Choose the word or phrase which is closest to OPPOSITE in meaning to the underlined word: They always displayed a <u>lackadaisical</u> approach to their studies so no one was surprised at the results.
questions.explanation: 'Lackadaisical' means lacking enthusiasm; the opposite is 'keen', meaning eager and enthusiastic.

distractors.D: 'Careless' is a near-synonym, not an antonym.

questions.stem: Choose the word or phrase which is closest to OPPOSITE in meaning to the underlined word: As a result of the available evidence, the accused was <u>apprehended</u> and no further action was taken.
questions.explanation: 'Apprehended' means arrested or taken into custody; its opposite is 'released'.

distractors.A: 'Evicted' means forced out of property, not the opposite of arrested.
distractors.C: 'Cautioned' means warned, not the opposite of arrested.
distractors.D: 'Summoned' means called to appear, not the opposite of apprehended.

questions.stem: Choose the word or pair of words which BEST completes the meaning of the sentence: After two years of probation he was .......... in the job because his employer found him to be an .......... worker.
questions.explanation: After a successful probation period, an employee is 'confirmed' (made permanent) because the employer found him to be an 'industrious' (hardworking) worker.

distractors.A: 'Resigned' would mean he quit, contradicting the positive outcome.
distractors.B: 'Retrenched' means dismissed due to staff reduction, contradicting the positive context.
distractors.D: 'Unskilled' is a negative trait that would not lead to retention.

questions.stem: Choose the word or pair of words which BEST completes the meaning of the sentence: When parents display a .......... for one child, the others are likely to feel ..........
questions.explanation: When parents show 'preference' (favouritism) for one child, the other children are likely to feel 'jealous'.

distractors.A: 'Concern' is positive and would not typically provoke unsympathetic feelings.
distractors.B: 'Dislike' towards one child does not logically lead to others being 'indifferent'.
distractors.C: 'Fondness' is loving, but 'lonely' is not the typical response of siblings.

questions.stem: Choose the word which BEST completes the meaning of the sentence: .......... people are likely to be afraid to take risks in life.
questions.explanation: 'Cautious' people are characteristically careful and reluctant to take risks.

distractors.A: 'Selfish' people may or may not avoid risks; selfishness is not directly linked to risk aversion.
distractors.D: 'Knowledgeable' people may take calculated risks confidently.

questions.stem: Choose the pair of words which BEST completes the meaning of the sentence: The journalists were impressed by the .......... of the statesman whose speeches had a profound .......... on all who heard them.
questions.explanation: 'Eloquence' (skilful speaking) of a statesman whose speeches had a profound 'effect' is the most natural collocation; speeches produce 'effects', and eloquence in speech is what impresses journalists.

distractors.A: 'Presence' and 'result' do not collocate naturally with speeches.
distractors.C: 'Emotion' is a feeling rather than a quality of the statesman.
distractors.D: 'Appearance' relates to looks, not speech quality.

questions.stem: Choose the word which BEST completes the meaning of the sentence: Are you trying to .......... that all along he knew of the plot to oust the committee?
questions.explanation: 'Insinuate' means to suggest indirectly, which fits a question implying that someone is hinting at hidden knowledge.

distractors.A: 'Clarify' means to make clear, the opposite of subtle suggestion.
distractors.C: 'Identify' means to recognise, not to suggest indirectly.

questions.stem: Choose the sentence NEAREST in meaning to: Much is being done to develop tourism in our country by providing good hotel accommodation and facilities.

options.A: It is important to provide good hotel accommodation and facilities for tourists.
options.B: Good hotel accommodation and facilities are crucial if we want to develop tourism.
options.C: We are doing a great deal to boost tourism by providing good hotel accommodation and facilities.
options.D: If we do not provide good hotel accommodation and facilities, tourists will not come to our country.

questions.explanation: The original says 'much is being done' (i.e., 'we are doing a great deal') 'to develop tourism' ('to boost tourism') by means of 'good hotel accommodation and facilities'. Option C preserves both the action and the means.

distractors.B: Expresses crucial conditions, not the action being taken.
distractors.D: Is a conditional, changing the meaning.

questions.stem: Choose the sentence NEAREST in meaning to: So engrossed was he in his task that he did not hear his sister enter the room.

options.A: His task was so difficult that he did not observe his sister's entry.
options.B: He did not hear his sister enter the room because of the job he was doing.
options.C: His sister's entry did not attract his attention because he was enjoying what he was doing.
options.D: He was so preoccupied with what he was doing that he was unaware of his sister's entry.

questions.explanation: 'Engrossed' means deeply preoccupied/absorbed. Option D captures this precisely: 'so preoccupied' that he was 'unaware of his sister's entry'.

distractors.B: Imprecise; the job alone, not absorption, is given as cause.
distractors.C: Says he was 'enjoying' the task, not that he was absorbed in it.

questions.stem: Choose the sentence NEAREST in meaning to: John told his parents in no uncertain terms that he was going to university as soon as he left school.

options.A: John boasted to his parents that he was going to university as soon as he left school.
options.B: John told his parents that they should permit him to go to university after he left school.
options.C: John made it quite clear to his parents that when he left school he would go to university immediately.
options.D: John's parents were told that it was obviously his desire to attend university immediately after leaving school.

questions.explanation: 'In no uncertain terms' means 'made it quite clear' - emphatically and unambiguously. Option C preserves both the firmness and the timing.

distractors.A: 'Boasted' adds an idea (showing off) not present in the original.
distractors.B: Changes the meaning to a request for permission.
distractors.D: Speaks of his 'desire', not his firm declaration.

questions.stem: Choose the sentence NEAREST in meaning to: The bright colours of the bird distinguished it from the others flying across the pale sky.

options.A: The bright colours of the bird contrasted with the pale sky while it was flying.
options.B: The pale sky made the birds noticeable while flying as they had bright colours.
options.C: The pale sky made the birds distinguished, particularly the one with the bright colours.
options.D: The bright colours against the pale sky made the bird stand out among those flying by.

questions.explanation: The original says one bird was distinguished (set apart) from the others by its bright colours against the pale sky. Option D conveys 'stood out among those flying by' - the same meaning.

distractors.A: Mentions contrast but omits 'from the others'.
distractors.B: Says 'birds' (plural) were noticeable, changing the meaning.
distractors.C: Makes all the birds distinguished, contradicting the original.

questions.stem: Choose the sentence NEAREST in meaning to: I cannot understand why you have done this since you tell me that Mark means a lot to you.

options.A: Since Mark means a lot to you, the reason for your action is puzzling to me.
options.B: Since you tell me that Mark means a lot to you, he cannot understand why you have done this.
options.C: You tell me that although Mark means a lot to you, you did this.
options.D: Mark cannot mean a lot to you or else I know you would not have done this.

questions.explanation: The original expresses puzzlement at the listener's action given that Mark matters to them. Option A retains this first-person puzzlement and reasoning.

distractors.B: Switches subject to 'he', changing the meaning.
distractors.C: Omits the speaker's inability to understand.
distractors.D: Concludes Mark does not matter, contradicting the original.

questions.stem: Select the ONE underlined part that is inappropriate; if acceptable, choose D. We could reach the survivors <u>neither</u>(A) by boat <u>or</u>(B) by plane, <u>and so we abandoned</u>(C) our search as futile. <u>No error</u>(D)
questions.explanation: 'Neither' must be paired with 'nor', not 'or'. The correct construction is 'neither by boat nor by plane'.

distractors.A: 'Neither' is correctly used here as part of the correlative conjunction.
distractors.C: 'And so we abandoned' is grammatically acceptable.
distractors.D: The sentence does contain an error, so 'No error' is wrong.

questions.stem: Select the ONE underlined part that is inappropriate; if acceptable, choose D. <u>I believe</u>(A) that if his attitude <u>improves</u>(B) his general performance <u>will also improve</u>(C). <u>No error</u>(D)
questions.explanation: The sentence is grammatically correct. The conditional uses the present tense in the if-clause and the future tense in the main clause appropriately.

distractors.B: Present tense 'improves' is correct in the if-clause.
distractors.C: Future tense 'will also improve' is correct in the main clause.

questions.stem: Select the ONE underlined part that is inappropriate; if acceptable, choose D. She was not feeling well <u>at the beginning</u>(A) of the journey, and <u>sitting at the front</u>(B) of the car made her <u>feel worse</u>(C). <u>No error</u>(D)
questions.explanation: The sentence is grammatically acceptable as it stands. All underlined parts are appropriate.

questions.stem: Select the ONE underlined part that is inappropriate; if acceptable, choose D. <u>Overcome by fear</u>(A), I ran <u>passed</u>(B) my home <u>without realizing</u>(C) that I had done so. <u>No error</u>(D)
questions.explanation: 'Passed' (past tense of the verb 'pass') is incorrect here. The correct word is 'past', the preposition meaning 'by/beyond'.

distractors.A: 'Overcome by fear' is a correctly formed participial phrase.
distractors.D: There is an error, so 'No error' is incorrect.

questions.stem: Select the ONE underlined part that is inappropriate; if acceptable, choose D. The better of the two athletes <u>claim</u>(A) <u>never to have</u>(B) used the dangerous drugs found <u>in his possession</u>(C). <u>No error</u>(D)
questions.explanation: The subject is 'the better' (singular), so the verb should be 'claims', not 'claim'. The singular pronoun 'his' later in the sentence confirms the subject is singular.

distractors.D: There is an error, so 'No error' is wrong.

questions.stem: Choose the word NEAREST IN MEANING to the underlined word: It was impossible to <u>rectify</u> the error made by the computer since inaccurate information had been fed into it.
questions.explanation: 'Rectify' means to put right or correct an error.

distractors.A: 'Alter' means to change but not necessarily to fix.
distractors.B: 'Erase' means to remove completely, not correct.
distractors.D: 'Determine' means to decide or establish, not to fix.

questions.stem: Choose the word NEAREST IN MEANING to the underlined word: The bank teller indicated that our account was <u>dormant</u>.
questions.explanation: 'Dormant' literally means sleeping/inactive; a dormant bank account is one with no recent activity.

distractors.D: 'Overdrawn' refers to a negative balance.

questions.stem: Choose the word NEAREST IN MEANING to the underlined word: The news story revealed the <u>squalor</u> of the old prison cells.
questions.explanation: 'Squalor' means extreme dirtiness, filth, or degraded conditions.

distractors.C: 'Ambiance' is a neutral term for atmosphere.

questions.stem: Choose the word NEAREST IN MEANING to the underlined word: A citizen is expected to show complete <u>allegiance</u> to his native land.
questions.explanation: 'Allegiance' most directly means loyalty or faithful support to a country.

distractors.B: 'Respect' is general regard, not the bond of loyalty.
distractors.C: 'Homage' is a public show of respect, narrower than allegiance.
distractors.D: 'Obedience' is compliance with rules, not the broader bond of loyalty.

questions.stem: Choose the word NEAREST IN MEANING to the underlined word: For the first time in a decade the company disclosed a <u>deficit</u> in its account.
questions.explanation: A 'deficit' in an account is a shortfall - the amount by which expenditure exceeds income.

distractors.A: 'Error' is a mistake, not a financial shortfall.
distractors.B: 'Defect' is a flaw or fault, not a financial term.
distractors.C: 'Balance' is the amount remaining, which could be positive or negative.

questions.stem:
There is a "single kite" (line 1) left in the sky MOST likely because

When a single kite is left
in evening sky
and I stand
black against its saffron
pulling in the night-covers
with a single thread
I think of you.

When chickens search the warmth
of a hen's wings;
When bats break a calm lake

questions.stem:
The mother's "thrust of the chin" (line 4) is done in

She had always been a small woman — short, light-boned, delicately structured — but now, under the white hospital sheet, she was becoming tiny. I thought of a doll with huge, fierce eyes. There had always been a fierceness in her. It showed in that angry, challenging thrust of the chin when she issued an opinion, and a great one she had always been for issuing opinions.

"I tell people exactly what's on my mind," she had been fond of boasting. "I tell them what I think, whether they like it or not." Often they had not liked it. She could be sarcastic to people in whom she detected evidence of the ignoramus or the fool.

"It's not always a good policy to tell people exactly what's on your mind," I used to caution her.

"If they don't like it, that's too bad," was her customary reply, "because that's the way I am."

And so she was. A formidable woman. Determined to speak her mind, determined to bend those who opposed her. In that time when I had known her best, my mother had hurled herself at life with chin thrust forward, eyes blazing, and an energy that made her seem always on the run.

She ran after squawking chickens, a knife in her hand, determined on the beheading that would put dinner in the pot. She ran when she made the beds, ran when she set the table. One Thanksgiving she burned herself badly when, running up from the cellar oven with the ceremonial turkey, she tripped on the stairs and tumbled back down ending on the bottom in the debris of giblets, hot gravy, and battered turkey. Life was combat, and victory was not for the lazy, the timid, the slugabed, the drugstore cowboy, the mushmouth afraid to tell people exactly what was on their mind whether people liked it or not. She ran.

But now her running was over.

Extract adapted from Russell Baker, "In My Day". Retrieved 23 February 2015 from http://www.douban.com/note/173685719.
questions.explanation: The 'angry, challenging thrust of the chin' is a posture of defiance — she will not back down.

distractors.D: Contemplation is passive.

questions.stem:
This passage is primarily based on

At one time, if you wanted a picture, you had to paint or draw it. Tourists took sketch pads, not cameras, on vacations. Cameras are a lot quicker and easier to use. Have you ever wondered how they came to be?

The first camera was used in the sixteenth century and was so large that the artist sat right inside it. The huge 'camera obscura', as it was called, was a clumsy instrument. It worked on the same principles as our cameras today. A tiny hole in one wall admitted light. On the opposite wall, an image was projected upside down. The artist would sit inside the camera and trace the image until the picture was done. In the 1800s, a demand for quick and inexpensive portraits led to the improvement of this camera.

Many inventors contributed to the improvement of the image and the camera itself. In the 1700s, the German physicist Johann Schulze discovered that an image can be made by exposing silver salts to light. The problem was that the image did not last. The first permanent image was obtained by Joseph Nicéphore Niépce, a French physicist. He exposed a metal plate to light, then engraved the image onto the plate. The earliest actual photograph still in existence was made in this way in 1826. It is a view from Niépce's room, and it took eight hours to expose!

A partner of Niépce, Louis J. M. Daguerre, invented the first practical photographic process. Exposing a silver plate to light, he made an image that did not have to be engraved. Instead, he created the image by using chemicals. After his invention was announced in 1839, modern photography took off.

The camera has come a long way from its beginnings. It is now possible for anyone to use a camera and get good, clear photographs. The inventions that made this possible are the result of many different minds working on the problem: first to get the image, then to make the image permanent, and finally to make the process practical. Next time you see a snapshot, remember the achievement of those early artists and inventors.

Passage on the history of the camera
questions.explanation: The passage gives dates, inventors and processes — historical facts about the camera's invention.

distractors.D: The passage states rather than infers.

Next time you see a snapshot, remember the achievement of those early artists and inventors.

Passage on the history of the camera
questions.explanation: The passage moves chronologically through inventors and improvements — logical order.

distractors.D: Past tense, not present.

questions.stem:
According to the writer, "modern photography took off" (lines 18–19) when

At one time, if you wanted a picture, you had to paint or draw it. Tourists took sketch pads, not cameras, on vacations. Cameras are a lot quicker and easier to use. Have you ever wondered how they came to be?

The first camera was used in the sixteenth century and was so large that the artist sat right inside it. The huge 'camera obscura', as it was called, was a clumsy instrument. It worked on the same principles as our cameras today. A tiny hole in one wall admitted light. On the opposite wall, an image was projected upside down. The artist would sit inside the camera and trace the image until the picture was done. In the 1800s, a demand for quick and inexpensive portraits led to the improvement of this camera.

Many inventors contributed to the improvement of the image and the camera itself. In the 1700s, the German physicist Johann Schulze discovered that an image can be made by exposing silver salts to light. The problem was that the image did not last. The first permanent image was obtained by Joseph Nicéphore Niépce, a French physicist. He exposed a metal plate to light, then engraved the image onto the plate. The earliest actual photograph still in existence was made in this way in 1826. It is a view from Niépce's room, and it took eight hours to expose!

A partner of Niépce, Louis J. M. Daguerre, invented the first practical photographic process. Exposing a silver plate to light, he made an image that did not have to be engraved. Instead, he created the image by using chemicals. After his invention was announced in 1839, modern photography took off.

The camera has come a long way from its beginnings. It is now possible for anyone to use a camera and get good, clear photographs. The inventions that made this possible are the result of many different minds working on the problem: first to get the image, then to make the image permanent, and finally to make the process practical. Next time you see a snapshot, remember the achievement of those early artists and inventors.

Passage on the history of the camera

options.C: Daguerre made his discovery by using chemicals
options.D: Schulze made his discovery by using silver salts

questions.explanation: The passage explicitly says modern photography took off after Daguerre announced his chemical-based process in 1839.

distractors.B: Niépce's photo didn't trigger the take-off.
distractors.D: Schulze's silver salts didn't last.

questions.stem:
The writer's use of the word "brain" (line 1) is meant to

This is an open request to try to find who is the 'brain' behind traffic management in Paolo. Does anybody know? I have tried Traffic Branch, not them; Highways, not them; Planning & Development, not them; in fact nobody wants to take responsibility for certain decisions and the mess that results.

For example, Mission Road was made one-way, north to south. Great idea! It worked, no traffic jams. In fact, many people were charged for going the wrong way up a one-way street. Then suddenly it's back to two-way and traffic for so! Same is true of Stoke Street. Somebody changed the 'Stop' sign at the corner for a few weeks, then they changed it back; nobody knows why.

But the best is Celestial Road in St John which is 'Wrecker Heaven'. Somebody made this road, which, apart from Broadway, is the widest street in Paolo, a 'no parking' street, either side, any time, and the 'Wrecker' is profiting from this.

There are four major office buildings on this road, all of which provide underground parking for staff, so some (usually the lower paid) have to park elsewhere and these are the persons whose cars the wrecker is taking away all day, everyday. Again the small man suffers!

Oh! there is an exception: if you have a Canadian High Commission sticker you can park and the wrecker will not touch your 'Diplomatic' car. Maybe we could all become Diplomats!

Then again, the two narrowest streets in Paolo allow parking; try Delino Street off Abby Lane, you can hardly pass!

Who is the 'brain' behind this mess? It seems the more cars we put on the road, the less parking somebody wants to allow. Who is this brain? No doubt a small man with a BIG, BIG car who has no name!

Letter on traffic management in "Paolo"
questions.explanation: The sarcastic tone — searching for the 'brain' behind such mess — mocks the decisions.

distractors.D: It mocks, not compliments.

questions.stem: The journalists were impressed by the ________ of the statesman whose speeches had a profound ________ on all who heard him.
questions.explanation: Eloquence (fluent persuasive speech) pairs naturally with the effect of speeches on listeners.

distractors.B: Presence and result do not collocate naturally with speeches.
distractors.C: Emotion and sensation do not fit a statesman's speech.
distractors.D: Appearance would not be linked to the content of speeches.

questions.stem: The new owners will be asked to start the plan as soon as possible and will be expected to ________ the policy of cost reduction approved by the board.
questions.explanation: To implement a policy means to carry it out, which fits the context of executing a board-approved plan.

distractors.A: State only means to declare; the policy is already approved.
distractors.B: Develop implies creating something new, but the policy already exists.
distractors.C: Frustrate means to block, which contradicts the context.

questions.stem: Although it is possible to move the house, the cost of moving it makes the project ________.
questions.explanation: High cost makes a project impractical (not feasible in practice).

distractors.C: Worthwhile would contradict the limiting nature of cost.
distractors.D: Inconsiderate refers to behaviour, not feasibility.

questions.stem: Choose the word or phrase that is most nearly OPPOSITE in meaning to the underlined words. She seems to be <u>sensitive to</u> the feelings of others.
questions.explanation: To be sensitive to feelings means to perceive and respond to them; the opposite is to be unaffected by them.

distractors.A: Unaware implies ignorance, not lack of response.
distractors.B: Unsure about implies uncertainty rather than the absence of feeling.
distractors.D: Appreciative of is similar in meaning, not opposite.

questions.stem: Choose the word or phrase that is most nearly OPPOSITE in meaning to the underlined word. Many teenagers and even some parents, do not agree with the level of <u>restrictiveness</u> in today's society.
questions.explanation: Restrictiveness means strictness; its opposite is permissiveness (allowing freedom).

distractors.D: Competitiveness is unrelated to restriction.

questions.stem: Choose the word or phrase that is most nearly OPPOSITE in meaning to the underlined word. He was <u>eager</u> to share the news with his friends.
questions.explanation: Eager means keen and willing; its opposite is reluctant (unwilling).

distractors.D: Unavailable indicates absence rather than unwillingness.

questions.stem: Choose the word or phrase that is most nearly OPPOSITE in meaning to the underlined word. The president of the students' association never mentioned the treasurer's <u>integrity</u>.
questions.explanation: Integrity means honesty and moral uprightness; its opposite is dishonesty.

distractors.B: Loyalty is a related virtue, not the opposite.

questions.stem: Choose the word or phrase that is most nearly OPPOSITE in meaning to the underlined word. While the employers approved of his work habits, they objected to his <u>radical</u> views.
questions.explanation: Radical views are extreme or unconventional; the opposite is conventional (traditional and accepted).

distractors.A: Capitalist describes an economic ideology, not a level of conformity.

questions.stem: Revise the sentence: "People from crowded nations cannot get over the accessibility of Canada's natural environment, in all of its magnificence." Begin with: "It is impossible for ________".
questions.explanation: After "It is impossible for [people]..." the infinitive form to get over is required.

distractors.C: To have got over is the perfect infinitive; not needed here.
distractors.D: To getting over is ungrammatical.

questions.stem: Revise the sentence: "On learning of the impending hurricane, many persons rushed to the supermarket." Begin with: "Many persons rushed ________".
questions.explanation: "On learning" expresses a moment in time, best rendered as when they learnt.

distractors.A: As they learned suggests simultaneous progress, not the moment of learning.
distractors.B: While they learnt changes meaning to a continuous action.
distractors.D: For they were learning is ungrammatical in context.

questions.stem: Revise the sentence: "More is experienced in one day of the life of a learned man than an ignorant man experiences in his whole lifetime." Begin with: "A learned man ________".

options.D: has more experience in one day

questions.explanation: The original uses the present tense ("is experienced", "experiences"). The transformation must preserve present tense: experiences more in one day.

distractors.B: More experienced in one day lacks a verb.
distractors.C: Experienced more in one day changes to past tense.
distractors.D: Has more experience shifts to a noun phrase; less faithful to the original verb structure.

questions.stem: Revise the sentence: "During my time at school I was involved in extracurricular activities." Begin with: "I was involved in extracurricular activities ________".
questions.explanation: While I was (at school) preserves the time-frame of "during my time at school".

distractors.D: When staying changes meaning and tense.

questions.stem: Revise the sentence: "'It's going to be alright,' Mum assured me." Begin with: "Mom assured me that ________".
questions.explanation: In reported speech, present tense "is going to" becomes past tense "was going to". Hence it was going to be alright.

distractors.B: There does not complete a grammatical clause.
distractors.C: Simply is an adverb, not a subject.
distractors.D: Only just is not a complete subject.

questions.stem: Identify the misspelt word, or choose D if no word is misspelt: "She is one of the (A) thousands of babies who are (B) diagnosed with that (C) disease. (D) No error."
questions.explanation: All three underlined words are correctly spelt; therefore, the answer is No error.

questions.stem: Identify the misspelt word, or choose D if no word is misspelt: "The (A) secretary had made up a (B) callendar of events but there was not enough (C) stationery to give each member a copy. (D) No error."
questions.explanation: Callendar is misspelt; the correct spelling is calendar.

distractors.D: There is an error, so this is incorrect.

questions.stem: Identify the misspelt or misused word, or choose D if no word is misspelt: "When the museum (A) purchased his paintings the (B) artist received many (C) complements. (D) No error."
questions.explanation: The intended word for praise is compliments, not complements (which means things that complete).

distractors.D: There is an error, so this is incorrect.

questions.stem: Identify the misspelt word, or choose D if no word is misspelt: "After (A) embarrassing his colleague his (B) conscience bothered him and he eventually (C) apologized. (D) No error."
questions.explanation: The word printed as 'embarrassiz' (in the original) is misspelt; the correct form is embarrassing.

distractors.D: There is an error, so this is incorrect.

questions.stem: Identify the misspelt or misused word, or choose D if no word is misspelt: "It is my (A) privilege to give you some (B) advise concerning proper (C) nutrition. (D) No error."
questions.explanation: The noun required after 'some' is advice; advise is the verb form, so it is misused here.

distractors.D: There is an error, so this is incorrect.

questions.stem: Choose the sentence NEAREST IN MEANING to the original: "So engrossed was he in his task that he did not hear his sister enter the room."

options.A: His task was so difficult that he did not observe his sister's entry.
options.B: He did not hear his sister enter the room because of the job he was doing.
options.C: His sister's entry did not attract his attention because he was enjoying what he was doing.
options.D: He was so preoccupied with what he was doing that he was unaware of his sister's entry.

questions.explanation: Engrossed means deeply absorbed or preoccupied; option D captures this meaning most accurately.

distractors.A: The original does not say the task was difficult, only absorbing.
distractors.B: This is too vague; it omits the depth of absorption.
distractors.C: Enjoyment is not stated; engrossment is about absorption, not pleasure.

questions.stem: Choose the sentence NEAREST IN MEANING to the original: "The news shocked me so much that I almost forgot what to say."

options.A: The news was so shocking that I could not move.
options.B: I could hardly remember my message after such shocking news.
options.C: I nearly didn't speak again after the shock of the news.
options.D: I was so shocked by the news that I could hardly speak.

questions.explanation: The original states the shock affected speech; D captures the meaning without overstating.

distractors.B: 'Message' is more specific than 'what to say'.
distractors.C: Suggests never speaking again, a stronger meaning.

questions.stem: Choose the sentence NEAREST IN MEANING to the original: "Recent floods have made the roads impassable."

options.A: People are unable to travel because of the floods.
options.B: Because of heavy showers, commuters were forced to detour.
options.C: The roads cannot be used as a result of the floods.
options.D: Travelling has been brought to a standstill because of the floods.

questions.explanation: Impassable means cannot be passed/used. C is the most precise restatement.

distractors.A: Goes too far; people may still travel by other means.
distractors.B: Mentions detours and heavy showers, not in the original.
distractors.D: 'Standstill' implies all travel stopped, an overstatement.

questions.stem: Choose the sentence NEAREST IN MEANING to the original: "The Europeans who were not well off were those who came to the West Indies in search of wealth."

options.A: Europeans settled in the West Indies because they were told they would become richer.
options.B: Unemployment at home caused Europeans to come to the West Indies to seek their fortune.
options.C: Financial difficulties at home made Europeans who came to the West Indies decide to settle there.
options.D: Europeans who came to the West Indies to seek their fortunes were the ones who were not wealthy.

questions.explanation: The original identifies which Europeans came to the West Indies: those who were not well off. D restates this most faithfully.

distractors.A: Adds the notion of being 'told'; not in original.
distractors.C: Speaks of settling, not in original.

questions.stem: Choose the sentence NEAREST IN MEANING to the original: "Water pollution is a common problem because people thoughtlessly dump their garbage in water courses."

options.A: People throw their garbage in water courses making this an unusual problem.
options.B: If people did not dump their garbage in water, we would not have a pollution problem.
options.C: Water pollution takes place because people negligently dump their garbage in water courses.
options.D: Even though water pollution is a problem, people still dump their garbage in water courses.

questions.explanation: Thoughtlessly is best matched by negligently, and C preserves the causal relationship.

distractors.D: Changes the relationship to concession, not cause.

questions.stem:
The speaker in the poem is

I have never learnt the names of flowers
From beginning, my world has been a place
Of pot-holed streets where thick, sluggish gutters race
In slow time, away from garbage heaps and sewers
Past blanched old houses around which cowers
Stagnant earth. There scarce green things grew to chase
The dull-grey squalor of sick dust; no trace
Of plant save few sparse weeds; just these, no flowers.

One day they cleared a space and made a park
There in the city's slums; and suddenly
Came stark glory like lightning in the dark.
While perfume and dark petals thundered slowly.
I learnt no names, but hue, shape and scent mark
My mind, even now, with symbols holy.

Poem: "Flowers" by Dennis Craig.
questions.explanation: The poet looks back — 'From beginning, my world has been a place...' and 'even now' — this is adult recollection.

distractors.C: The 'even now' marker shows it is an adult voice.

options.A: Nature is a vital part of human life.
options.B: The impact of nature is positive and deep.
options.C: The names of plants are unimportant.
options.D: Nature, specifically parks, should be introduced in all slums.

distractors.D: A literal policy lesson the poem doesn't give.

questions.stem:
Which word BEST describes the homeowner's attitude towards the hillside near his house?

Marcus wheeled himself out to his accustomed place at the table on the terrace and the sight struck him immediately. How could it not? It was an abomination, a desecration, a heresy, a sight unbelievable. There was a house on the hillside! His hill, the one which overlooked the village and which his terrace faced. It was a small house to be sure; he estimated it to be no more than twenty feet by twelve, with one door and one window on the long side which faced him. From the distance, it was no more than a doll's house, or what a child might draw; a wooden hut similar to those which sprung up daily in the squatter settlements which everywhere littered the hillsides, ringing the city, visible evidence of society out of control. But his hillside wasn't a squatter settlement and no other house marred his view. His house sat on a slight rise above the village in the valley, and from there he had an uninterrupted view of the mountains on all sides. Far away there were houses, proper ones, that is; but none close enough to mar his vision.

It had never occurred to him that the hillside he gazed at every day would ever be defaced with human habitation. It was so steep and inhospitable. Few trees grew on it except for the occasional clump of mangoes, it was mostly covered with grass which burned every year in the dry months and agaves which from time to time flowered yellow. Half way up the hill and directly in front of his favourite seat on the terrace was the only tree of any size and this was a silk-cotton tree which would one day become a giant but which was still small as cotton trees go. Year after year he watched its progress as it shed its leaves in the winter months and turned a bright green in the spring. And now immediately behind this tree, as if sheltering in its arms, someone had built a house. On his hill. It wasn't really his hill, but who else was there to claim it, who else had gazed at it three hundred and sixty-five mornings every year for the past twelve years?

Extract: "The View from the Terrace"
questions.explanation: Marcus repeatedly calls it 'his hill' even though it isn't legally his — that is possessive.

distractors.A: Anger comes after; underlying attitude is possessive.

It wasn't really his hill, but who else was there to claim it, who else had gazed at it three hundred and sixty-five mornings every year for the past twelve years?

Extract: "The View from the Terrace"

options.A: The silk-cotton tree would become gigantic.
options.B: Proper houses would be built on the hillside.
options.C: The hillside would become a squatters' settlement.
options.D: Littering would become a problem on the hillside.

questions.explanation: Marcus likens the new hut to those in 'squatter settlements' that 'littered the hillsides'.

distractors.A: The tree growing is observed, not feared.
distractors.D: 'Littered' is metaphorical.

questions.stem:
The repetition of "his hill" (lines 3, 20) by the narrator

Marcus wheeled himself out to his accustomed place at the table on the terrace and the sight struck him immediately. How could it not? It was an abomination, a desecration, a heresy, a sight unbelievable. There was a house on the hillside! His hill, the one which overlooked the village and which his terrace faced. It was a small house to be sure; he estimated it to be no more than twenty feet by twelve, with one door and one window on the long side which faced him. From the distance, it was no more than a doll's house, or what a child might draw; a wooden hut similar to those which sprung up daily in the squatter settlements which everywhere littered the hillsides, ringing the city, visible evidence of society out of control. But his hillside wasn't a squatter settlement and no other house marred his view. His house sat on a slight rise above the village in the valley, and from there he had an uninterrupted view of the mountains on all sides. Far away there were houses, proper ones, that is; but none close enough to mar his vision.

It had never occurred to him that the hillside he gazed at every day would ever be defaced with human habitation. It was so steep and inhospitable. Few trees grew on it except for the occasional clump of mangoes, it was mostly covered with grass which burned every year in the dry months and agaves which from time to time flowered yellow. Half way up the hill and directly in front of his favourite seat on the terrace was the only tree of any size and this was a silk-cotton tree which would one day become a giant but which was still small as cotton trees go. Year after year he watched its progress as it shed its leaves in the winter months and turned a bright green in the spring. And now immediately behind this tree, as if sheltering in its arms, someone had built a house. On his hill. It wasn't really his hill, but who else was there to claim it, who else had gazed at it three hundred and sixty-five mornings every year for the past twelve years?

Extract: "The View from the Terrace"

options.A: displays the depth of his disgust
options.B: shows how persistent his shock was
options.D: emphasizes the passion behind his attitude

questions.explanation: The repetition underlines the intensity of his emotional claim over the hill.

distractors.C: Refers to his view being spoiled.

questions.stem:
According to paragraph 1 (lines 1–5), which of the following results from conflict between family members?

When the traditional family unit is discussed, it is usually in terms of the external social changes that are threatening its existence as an institution. Little thought is given to the internal problems of normal homes. The central problem for most family members is, of course, how to get along with each other. This internal matter is not without its external implications; for only where there are orderly and peaceful families can there be an orderly and peaceful society.

Of all the social changes that have affected the family in recent years, by far the most significant has been the increase in the number of mothers of school-age children who have taken outside employment. In Canada, some 75 percent of women in this category now have full-time or part-time jobs. For the most part, economic imperatives have left women no choice but to work for money. An income sufficient to maintain the lifestyle of an average family took one Canadian 48 hours a week to earn in the 1950s. It takes two people 65 to 75 hours a week to earn that income today.

The conflicts between work and family life and scarcity of time to devote to children have taken a personal toll on women in the form of stress and depression. Obviously, individuals under stress are harder to deal with than those who are not, so the tensions of work are carried over into tensions in mother–child relationships. Men, too, report feeling 'stressed out' and squeezed between work and family obligations. Males raised in the tradition of mothers doing everything in the home are inclined to be lax in doing housework and awkward in the unaccustomed role of actively nurturing children. But if a two-income family is to run smoothly and fairly, the household workload must be shared.

Another profound change in family relationships lies in the relatively high incidence of divorce and marital separations in Western society. The fact that so many couples feel they must go their separate ways illustrates just how difficult it is for people to live together satisfactorily at the best of times.

Passage on the family unit

options.D: High levels of tension between parents

questions.explanation: The paragraph closes: 'for only where there are orderly and peaceful families can there be an orderly and peaceful society' — family conflict leads to societal disorder.

distractors.C: Disruptive behaviour is cause, not result.
distractors.D: Tension between parents isn't in paragraph 1.

options.A: It hides possible consequences of using the product.
options.B: It suggests that the side effects are minor.

options.D: It demonstrates that the problems are minor in comparison to the benefits.
