options.A: enticing the customers to save money
options.B: persuading customers to buy the product quickly
options.C: prompting Caribbean people to go in search of the product
options.D: fooling the prospective buyers into thinking he/she needs the product

questions.explanation: 'While stocks last' creates urgency — buy now before it's gone.

distractors.A: Saving is implied by the slashed price.
distractors.D: Triggers urgency, not deception about need.

questions.stem: Choose the word closest to OPPOSITE in meaning to the underlined word. The Manager decided to call a meeting to inform the staff about the <u>complaints</u> received from customers.
questions.explanation: The antonym of 'complaints' (negative feedback) is 'compliments' (positive feedback/praise).

distractors.A: Requests are neutral asks, not the opposite of complaints.
distractors.B: Demands are forceful requests, similar in negative tone.
distractors.C: Suggestions are neutral proposals, not opposite in meaning.

questions.stem: Choose the word closest to OPPOSITE in meaning to the underlined word. Their actions showed that they were firm in their <u>rejection</u> of the new law.
questions.explanation: The antonym of 'rejection' (refusal) is 'acceptance' (agreement to receive or approve).

distractors.A: Ignorance refers to a lack of knowledge, not the opposite of rejection.
distractors.B: Application refers to use or implementation, not opposite to rejection.
distractors.D: Understanding refers to comprehension, not the opposite of rejection.

questions.stem: Choose the word closest to OPPOSITE in meaning to the underlined word. There was an <u>increase</u> in airplane travel after that incident.
questions.explanation: The antonym of 'increase' (rise) is 'decline' (decrease/fall).

distractors.B: Upheaval refers to disruption, not a decrease.
distractors.C: Restriction means limitation, not the opposite of increase.
distractors.D: Fluctuation means variation up and down, not opposite to increase.

questions.stem: Choose the word closest to OPPOSITE in meaning to the underlined word. He tried to think of a <u>tactful</u> way to tell her he disagreed with her view of the issue.
questions.explanation: 'Tactful' means showing sensitivity to avoid offense. Its opposite is 'injurious' meaning hurtful or harmful.

distractors.A: Honest is not the opposite of tactful; one can be both honest and tactful.
distractors.C: Indifferent means showing no concern; not strictly the opposite of tactful.
distractors.D: Considerate is a synonym of tactful, not its opposite.

questions.stem: Choose the word closest to OPPOSITE in meaning to the underlined word. As a result of the available evidence, the accused was <u>apprehended</u> and no further action was taken.
questions.explanation: 'Apprehended' means arrested or taken into custody. Its opposite is 'released' (set free).

distractors.A: Evicted means forced out (from a property), not the opposite of arrested.
distractors.C: Cautioned means warned, not opposite to apprehended.
distractors.D: Summoned means called to appear, not the opposite of apprehended.

questions.stem: Select the option that BEST describes the sentence. He advanced a step or two to meet his enemies, but they cowered, alarmed, and retreated quickly.

options.A: The sentence is too wordy, that is, repetitive or contains redundancies.
options.B: The sentence contains clichés or misused metaphors.
options.C: The sentence is incorrect grammatically or faulty in diction.
options.D: The sentence is acceptable as it stands.

questions.explanation: The sentence is grammatically correct and free of redundancy or cliché.

distractors.A: There is no redundancy in the sentence.
distractors.B: No clichés or misused metaphors appear here.

questions.stem: Select the option that BEST describes the sentence. It's not absolutely necessary for it to be done today.

options.A: The sentence is too wordy, that is, repetitive or contains redundancies.
options.B: The sentence contains clichés or misused metaphors.
options.C: The sentence is incorrect grammatically or faulty in diction.
options.D: The sentence is acceptable as it stands.

questions.explanation: 'Absolutely necessary' is redundant since 'necessary' already implies absoluteness. The sentence is wordy.

distractors.B: There is no cliché or misused metaphor here.
distractors.D: The sentence contains a redundancy and is not acceptable as it stands.

questions.stem: Select the option that BEST describes the sentence. He burnt the midnight oil to prepare for the interview.

options.A: The sentence is too wordy, that is, repetitive or contains redundancies.
options.B: The sentence contains clichés or misused metaphors.
options.C: The sentence is incorrect grammatically or faulty in diction.
options.D: The sentence is acceptable as it stands.

questions.explanation: 'Burnt the midnight oil' is a well-known cliché meaning to study/work late.

distractors.D: The use of cliché makes it not fully acceptable.

questions.stem: Select the option that BEST describes the sentence. The new disciplinary methods had a positive affect on the students' behaviour.

options.A: The sentence is too wordy, that is, repetitive or contains redundancies.
options.B: The sentence contains clichés or misused metaphors.
options.C: The sentence is incorrect grammatically or faulty in diction.
options.D: The sentence is acceptable as it stands.

questions.explanation: The word 'affect' (verb) is used incorrectly. It should be 'effect' (noun) in this context. This is faulty diction.

distractors.B: There are no clichés or misused metaphors.
distractors.D: The diction error makes it unacceptable.

questions.stem: Select the option that BEST describes the sentence. Sitting alone in his room by himself he repeated her name over and over again.

options.A: The sentence is too wordy, that is, repetitive or contains redundancies.
options.B: The sentence contains clichés or misused metaphors.
options.C: The sentence is incorrect grammatically or faulty in diction.
options.D: The sentence is acceptable as it stands.

questions.explanation: 'Alone... by himself' is redundant, and 'over and over again' is also repetitive. The sentence contains redundancies.

distractors.B: There are no clichés or misused metaphors.
distractors.D: The redundancies make it not acceptable.

questions.stem: Choose the sentence NEAREST in meaning to the original sentence. Much is being done to develop tourism in our country by providing good hotel accommodation and facilities.

options.A: It is important to provide good hotel accommodation and facilities for tourism to develop.
options.B: Good hotel accommodation and facilities are crucial if we want to develop tourism.
options.C: We are doing a great deal to boost tourism by providing good hotel accommodation and facilities.
options.D: If we do not provide good hotel accommodation and facilities, tourists will not come to our country.

questions.explanation: Option C preserves the original meaning: ongoing actions ('much is being done' = 'doing a great deal') aimed at developing tourism through good accommodation and facilities.

distractors.A: States importance, not what is being done.
distractors.B: States the necessity, not the action being taken.
distractors.D: Suggests a conditional/consequence not in the original.

questions.stem: Choose the sentence NEAREST in meaning to the original sentence. According to the witness, he did not see the accused man near the scene of the crime.

options.A: The witness testified that he did not see the accused man commit the crime.
options.B: The witness testified that the accused man committed the crime unless he was nearby.
options.C: The witness testified to seeing no evidence that the accused had committed the crime.
options.D: The witness testified that the accused man was far from the place where the crime was committed.

questions.explanation: Option D best preserves the meaning: the witness said the accused was not near (was far from) the crime scene.

distractors.A: Says he did not see the accused commit the crime, which is different from not seeing him near the scene.
distractors.B: Adds a conditional and asserts the accused committed the crime.
distractors.C: Refers to evidence about commission, not proximity to the scene.

questions.stem: Choose the sentence NEAREST in meaning to the original sentence. John told his parents in no uncertain terms that he would go to university as soon as he left school.

options.A: John asked his parents for permission to go to university as soon as he left school.
options.B: John was given the assurance that they would permit him to go to university after he left school.
options.C: John made it quite clear to his parents that when he left school he would go to university immediately.
options.D: John's parents were told that it was obviously his desire to attend university immediately after leaving school.

questions.explanation: 'In no uncertain terms' means very clearly. Option C captures this: John made it quite clear that he would go to university right after leaving school.

distractors.A: He told them, he didn't ask permission.
distractors.B: Reverses the situation: he was the one telling, not being told.
distractors.D: Uses 'obviously his desire' which softens the firm declaration of the original.

questions.stem: Choose the sentence NEAREST in meaning to the original sentence. Cigarettes have been linked to lung cancer and, strangely enough, so has bacon.

options.A: It is strange that bacon, quite unlike cigarettes, is linked to lung cancer.
options.B: It is rather peculiar that bacon, like cigarettes, is connected with lung cancer.
options.C: It is no wonder at all that both cigarettes and bacon are linked to lung cancer.
options.D: Cancer of the lungs links cigarettes, and, strangely enough, bacon.

questions.explanation: The original expresses surprise that bacon, like cigarettes, is associated with lung cancer. Option B captures this with 'peculiar' (= strange) and the link to lung cancer.

distractors.A: Says 'quite unlike' which contradicts the original meaning.
distractors.C: Says 'no wonder at all' which is the opposite of 'strangely enough'.
distractors.D: Awkward and reverses the linking relationship.

questions.stem: Choose the sentence NEAREST in meaning to the original sentence. I cannot understand why you have done this since you tell me that Mark means a lot to you.

options.A: Since Mark means a lot to you, the reason for your action is puzzling to me.
options.B: Since you tell me that Mark means a lot to you, he cannot understand why you have done this.
options.C: You tell me that although Mark means a lot to you, you did this.
options.D: Mark cannot mean a lot to you or else I know you would not have done this.

questions.explanation: Option A preserves the original meaning: because Mark is important to you, the speaker finds the action puzzling/cannot understand it.

distractors.C: Does not convey the speaker's inability to understand.
distractors.D: Asserts Mark doesn't mean a lot, contradicting the premise.

questions.stem: Select the ONE underlined part that is inappropriate; if the sentence is acceptable as it stands, choose D. We could reach the survivors (A) <u>neither by boat or</u> by plane, (B) <u>and so</u> we (C) <u>abandoned</u> our search activities. (D) No error
questions.explanation: The correlative conjunction 'neither' must pair with 'nor', not 'or'. It should be 'neither by boat nor by plane'.

distractors.B: 'And so' is acceptable to express consequence.
distractors.D: There is an error in the sentence.

questions.stem: Select the ONE underlined part that is inappropriate; if the sentence is acceptable as it stands, choose D. (A) <u>Considering</u> the chaos you would cause if you were to resign, I have (B) <u>no choice than</u> to refuse your (C) <u>request</u>. (D) No error
questions.explanation: The correct idiom is 'no choice but to', not 'no choice than to'.

distractors.D: There is an error in the sentence.

questions.stem: Select the ONE underlined part that is inappropriate; if the sentence is acceptable as it stands, choose D. Tom, the (A) <u>most talented</u> of the musicians who (B) <u>need</u> help, has promised to give his services (C) <u>free of charge</u> for any worthwhile cause. (D) No error
questions.explanation: The sentence is grammatically correct. 'Most talented' is the correct superlative; 'need' agrees with the plural 'musicians'; 'free of charge' is a standard idiom.

distractors.A: 'Most talented' is correctly used as a superlative.
distractors.C: 'Free of charge' is a standard expression.

questions.stem: Select the ONE underlined part that is inappropriate; if the sentence is acceptable as it stands, choose D. His (A) <u>conscience</u> finally (B) <u>bothered him</u> and he (C) <u>eventually</u> apologized. (D) No error
questions.explanation: The use of 'finally' and 'eventually' together is redundant since both express the same idea of something happening after delay.

distractors.D: The redundancy is an error.

questions.stem: Select the ONE underlined part that is inappropriate; if the sentence is acceptable as it stands, choose D. The (A) <u>better</u> of the two suspects (B) <u>claim</u> never to have used the dangerous drugs (C) <u>found in his possession</u>. (D) No error
questions.explanation: 'Claim' should be 'claims' to agree with the singular subject 'the better of the two suspects'.

distractors.D: There is a subject-verb agreement error.

questions.stem: Choose the word NEAREST IN MEANING to the underlined word. It was important to <u>rectify</u> the error made by the clerk before the information had spread further.
questions.explanation: 'Rectify' means to put right or correct.

distractors.A: Alter means to change, not necessarily to correct.
distractors.B: Erase means to remove completely, not to correct.
distractors.D: Determine means to decide or establish, not to fix an error.

questions.stem: Choose the word NEAREST IN MEANING to the underlined word. The flowers grow <u>profusely</u> in the garden.
questions.explanation: 'Profusely' means in great quantity or abundance.

distractors.B: Healthily refers to good health, not quantity.
distractors.D: Colourfully refers to colour, not quantity.

questions.stem: Choose the word NEAREST IN MEANING to the underlined word. The news story revealed the <u>squalor</u> of the old city.
questions.explanation: 'Squalor' refers to a state of extreme dirt and filth, particularly due to neglect or poverty.

distractors.A: Position refers to location, not state.
distractors.C: Ambiance refers to atmosphere, not filth.
distractors.D: Destitution means extreme poverty, related but not the same as filth.

questions.stem: Choose the word NEAREST IN MEANING to the underlined word. During the rainstorm yesterday the wind blew <u>incessantly</u> across the island.
questions.explanation: 'Incessantly' means without interruption or constantly.

distractors.A: Noisily refers to sound, not duration.
distractors.B: Furiously refers to intensity, not continuity.
distractors.D: Occasionally is opposite in meaning.

questions.stem: Choose the word NEAREST IN MEANING to the underlined word. For the first time in a decade the company disclosed a <u>deficit</u> in its account.
questions.explanation: 'Deficit' means a shortfall — an amount by which something falls short, especially money.

distractors.A: Profit is the opposite of deficit.
distractors.B: Defect means a flaw, not a financial shortfall.
distractors.C: Balance is the amount remaining, not the shortfall.

questions.stem:
There is a "single kite" (line 1) left in the sky MOST likely because

When a single kite is left
in evening sky
and I stand
black against its saffron
pulling in the night-covers
with a single thread
I think of you.

When chickens search the warmth
of a hen's wings;
When bats break a calm lake
like scissors on a warm fabric
where a lone fisherman waits
alone, on the hill,
I think of you.

Poem: "Taste of Endless Fruit"

options.D: the other kite flyers are asleep

questions.explanation: The evening sky is being 'pulled in' for the night — it is late.

distractors.B: The fisherman is a separate image.
distractors.C: Night covers are still being pulled in.
distractors.D: Not stated in the poem.

questions.stem:
Which of the following literary devices is used in line 4 ("black against its saffron")?

When a single kite is left
in evening sky
and I stand
black against its saffron
pulling in the night-covers
with a single thread
I think of you.

When chickens search the warmth
of a hen's wings;
When bats break a calm lake
like scissors on a warm fabric
where a lone fisherman waits

questions.stem:
The activity described in the passage is

The Coronation Carnival had waxed hectic and heated. When Lance Corporal John had left the station early and walked out onto the main road he realized that the commotion he had been hearing was from a band coming along the Avocat Road towards the junction down at the bridge. In the first sunlight of morning he saw people jumping up and making merry, and he saw people waving branches and green twigs and the men in front were beating spoon on bottle and there were some with lengths of bamboo keeping up a thick, steady rhythm. He noticed about six men in front banging on big oil drums and pitch-oil tins, and jumping high, dancing, and there were others thumping on even the buckets with which they went to the wells for water. For these revellers it seemed a truly glorious morning, and as he climbed the bank to watch them he heard the chorus of voices from behind blending with the drums. For the first time since he joined the police force he looked at reckless, celebrating crowds without wanting to make an arrest.

He stood high up on a mound, above the drain, to allow the free flow of the band, and he strained a little to hear which patriotic song the band was singing. He was wondering if it was 'Land of Hope and Glory', an anthem which usually brought tears to his eyes. The sound of spoon on bottle, of bamboo on macadam* road and of the rattling of tin, blurred the voices a little, and he knew he had to wait until the front of the band got past him.

He watched the women wearing housecoats and carrying banners — which he could not see clearly — and there came the powerful, strident voices of a group led by none other than Swithin Paul.

*macadam — tar/asphalt

Extract adapted from Michael Anthony, "Butler, Till the Final Bell", Macmillan Caribbean, 2004, p. 116.
questions.explanation: The passage opens: 'The Coronation Carnival had waxed hectic and heated.' It is a carnival celebration.

distractors.D: The carnival is not unusual.

options.A: that it was a bright and sunny day

questions.stem:
Casinos and their effect on society became an issue for the first time

More than a quarter century ago, we were forced to wrestle with the thorny issue of casinos and the supposed ills and benefits which flow from them. In the end, a sensible solution emerged: allow some to remain while controlling their growth. Interestingly, we may argue about casinos, but we have given the green light to arcades where people gamble — day and night.

Some Caribbean governments have seemingly opened the door to yet another round of possible emotional debate with the decision to allow cruise ships lying at anchor to operate their casinos in their waters. Is this move a prelude to opening casinos on land?

Supporters of casino gambling have always said that casinos can and do function in a country without the moral decay that is routinely associated with them. Like most things, the pros and the cons about casino gambling are grossly exaggerated.

Those who favour them argue that casinos on land should be seen as another attraction that can be added to the country's tourism industry, if prudent controls are imposed. For example, The Bahamas, Antigua, Suriname and Sint Maarten are a few places where casino gambling is allowed and tourism authorities in those places have said that casino gambling has proven to be a stimulus to their tourism sector, without the moral decay that is routinely associated with them.

Opponents, on the other hand, insist that casinos lead to gambling addiction, crime, and worse, a debasement of society. With everything else that is happening in our islands — an upsurge of gangs, drugs and violent behaviour among our youth — that is the last thing that we may need right now. However, the case of the cruise ships keeping casinos open while docked on shore may cause little if any harm to the country and its people since few locals will have access to them.

Let's face it, Caribbean people gamble at bingo to raise money for churches; they flock to the tracks to watch horses run and bet millions annually on the outcome of the races. Additionally, many play the lotto daily and the English football pools weekly.

So if, as some are saying, this move of allowing gambling on cruise ships in port may be the first step to casinos on land, then we will not be totally surprised.

So let the debates begin.

Article: "Are Casinos Becoming a Reality?" Daily Nation, 26 April 2010, p. 8.
questions.explanation: 'More than a quarter century ago' = over 25 years ago.

questions.stem:
The writer's use of the word "brain" (line 1) is meant to

This is an open request to try to find who is the 'brain' behind traffic management in Paolo. Does anybody know? I have tried Traffic Branch, not them; Highways, not them; Planning & Development, not them; in fact nobody wants to take responsibility for certain decisions and the mess that results.

For example, Mission Road was made one-way, north to south. Great idea! It worked, no traffic jams. In fact, many people were charged for going the wrong way up a one-way street. Then suddenly it's back to two-way and traffic for so! Same is true of Stoke Street. Somebody changed the 'Stop' sign at the corner for a few weeks, then they changed it back; nobody knows why.

But the best is Celestial Road in St John which is 'Wrecker Heaven'. Somebody made this road, which, apart from Broadway, is the widest street in Paolo, a 'no parking' street, either side, any time, and the 'Wrecker' is profiting from this.

There are four major office buildings on this road, all of which provide underground parking for staff, so some (usually the lower paid) have to park elsewhere and these are the persons whose cars the wrecker is taking away all day, everyday. Again the small man suffers!

Oh! there is an exception: if you have a Canadian High Commission sticker you can park and the wrecker will not touch your 'Diplomatic' car. Maybe we could all become Diplomats!

Then again, the two narrowest streets in Paolo allow parking; try Delino Street off Abby Lane, you can hardly pass!

Who is the 'brain' behind this mess? It seems the more cars we put on the road, the less parking somebody wants to allow. Who is this brain? No doubt a small man with a BIG, BIG car who has no name!

Letter on traffic management in "Paolo"
questions.explanation: 'Brain' is sarcastic — the writer mocks the decisions.

distractors.D: Exaggeration isn't its function.

questions.stem:
What does this airline promise if a person joins the Miles programme?

JOIN OUR CARIBBEAN MILES PROGRAMME

You can earn Caribbean Airlines Miles every flight; plus when you fly, drive or shop, you will earn miles even faster.

OUR MEMBERSHIP:
• Members who fly 20 000 miles on Caribbean Airlines within a calendar year are eligible for a Silver Membership.
• Flying 40 000 miles qualifies members for a Gold Membership, while flying 80 000 miles qualifies members for an Executive Gold Membership.

SILVER TIER MEMBERSHIP
• Unique member credentials
• A 3 000 Miles Bonus upon attainment of Silver Tier Status
• Preferential check-in and priority boarding on all Caribbean Airlines flights
• Waiver of date change penalty and redeposit fee on reward tickets

GOLD TIER MEMBERSHIP
• Unique member credentials
• A 5 000 Miles Bonus upon attainment of Gold Tier Status
• Preferential check-in and priority boarding on all Caribbean Airlines flights
• Waiver of date change penalty and redeposit fee on reward tickets
• Expedited award delivery at no cost

DESTINATION / AWARDS / MILES NEEDED
Within the Caribbean or between South America* and the Caribbean:
One Way Upgrade — 5 000
One Way Economy Class — 7 500
One Way Business Class — 12 000
Between the Caribbean / South America* and North America:
One Way Upgrade — 10 000
One Way Economy Class — 15 500
One Way Business Class — 25 500
Between Kingston and North America:
One Way Economy Class — 16 250
One Way Business Class — 28 750

* South America refers to Caracas, Georgetown & Paramaribo.

Thank you for flying with Caribbean Airlines.
Visit us at caribbean-airlines.com or call us.
E-mail: miles@caribbean-airlines.com

Advertisement adapted from "Caribbean Miles", Caribbean Beat, No. 108, March/April 2011, Media and Education Projects, p. 91.
questions.explanation: The advertisement states members 'can earn Caribbean Airline Miles every flight'.

questions.stem: Choose the word which is nearly OPPOSITE in meaning to the underlined word. The manager decided to inform the staff about all <u>complaints</u> received from customers.
questions.explanation: The antonym of 'complaints' (expressions of dissatisfaction) is 'compliments' (expressions of praise).

distractors.A: A request is a neutral asking, not the opposite of a complaint.
distractors.B: A demand is a forceful request, similar in negative tone.
distractors.C: A suggestion is a neutral recommendation, not the antonym.

questions.stem: Choose the word which is nearly OPPOSITE in meaning to the underlined word. There was an <u>increase</u> in airplane travel after that incident.
questions.explanation: The antonym of 'increase' is 'decline', meaning a decrease.

distractors.C: 'Restriction' is a limitation, not the opposite of increase.
distractors.D: 'Fluctuation' implies variation, not specifically a decrease.

questions.stem: Choose the word which is nearly OPPOSITE in meaning to the underlined word. <u>Constructive</u> criticism is neither appreciated nor encouraged by some of our peers.
questions.explanation: 'Constructive' (helpful, aimed at improvement) is opposed by 'negative' (destructive, fault-finding).

distractors.D: 'Unsatisfactory' describes quality but is not a clean antonym of constructive.

questions.stem: Choose the word which is nearly OPPOSITE in meaning to the underlined word. He tried to think of a <u>tactful</u> way to tell her he disagreed with her view of the situation.
questions.explanation: 'Tactful' (diplomatic and sensitive) is the opposite of 'impolite' (rude and inconsiderate).

distractors.A: 'Honest' is not the opposite; one can be both honest and tactful.
distractors.D: 'Extraordinary' means unusual, unrelated to tactfulness.

questions.stem: Choose the word which is nearly OPPOSITE in meaning to the underlined word. They always displayed a <u>lackadaisical</u> approach to their studies so no one was surprised at the results.
questions.explanation: 'Lackadaisical' (lacking enthusiasm and effort) is opposed by 'diligent' (hard-working, careful).

distractors.A: 'Calm' refers to a peaceful disposition, not the opposite of lackadaisical.
distractors.D: 'Careless' is similar in meaning to lackadaisical.

questions.stem: Select the option that BEST describes the sentence. 'He advanced a step or two to meet his attacker who became alarmed and retreated back.'

options.A: The sentence is too wordy, that is, repetitive or contains redundancies.
options.B: The sentence contains cliches or misused metaphors.
options.C: The sentence is incorrect grammatically or faulty in diction.
options.D: The sentence is acceptable as it stands.

questions.explanation: 'Retreated back' is redundant since 'retreat' already means to move back.

distractors.B: There is no cliche or misused metaphor.
distractors.C: The grammar itself is fine; the issue is redundancy.
distractors.D: The sentence is unacceptable due to the redundancy.

questions.stem: Select the option that BEST describes the sentence. 'Its not necessary for it to be done today.'

options.A: The sentence is too wordy, that is, repetitive or contains redundancies.
options.B: The sentence contains cliches or misused metaphors.
options.C: The sentence is incorrect grammatically or faulty in diction.
options.D: The sentence is acceptable as it stands.

questions.explanation: The sentence is grammatically incorrect: 'Its' should be 'It's' (contraction of 'it is').

distractors.B: There is no cliche or misused metaphor.
distractors.D: The sentence is unacceptable due to the missing apostrophe.

questions.stem: Select the option that BEST describes the sentence. 'She had adequate time to prepare for the interview.'

options.A: The sentence is too wordy, that is, repetitive or contains redundancies.
options.B: The sentence contains cliches or misused metaphors.
options.C: The sentence is incorrect grammatically or faulty in diction.
options.D: The sentence is acceptable as it stands.

questions.explanation: The sentence is grammatically correct, concise, and contains no clichés or redundancies.

distractors.A: The sentence is concise with no redundancy.
distractors.B: There is no cliche or misused metaphor.
distractors.C: The grammar and diction are correct.

questions.stem: Select the option that BEST describes the sentence. 'The new disciplinary methods had a positive affect on the students' behaviour.'

options.A: The sentence is too wordy, that is, repetitive or contains redundancies.
options.B: The sentence contains cliches or misused metaphors.
options.C: The sentence is incorrect grammatically or faulty in diction.
options.D: The sentence is acceptable as it stands.

questions.explanation: The sentence is faulty in diction: 'affect' (verb) is used where the noun 'effect' is required.

distractors.A: The sentence is not wordy or redundant.
distractors.B: There is no cliche or misused metaphor.
distractors.D: The sentence is unacceptable due to the affect/effect confusion.

questions.stem: Select the option that BEST describes the sentence. 'Sitting alone in his room by himself he repeated her name over and over again.'

options.A: The sentence is too wordy, that is, repetitive or contains redundancies.
options.B: The sentence contains cliches or misused metaphors.
options.C: The sentence is incorrect grammatically or faulty in diction.
options.D: The sentence is acceptable as it stands.

questions.explanation: 'Alone... by himself' and 'over and over again' are redundant: each pair repeats the same idea.

distractors.B: The sentence does not contain a cliche or misused metaphor.
distractors.C: The grammar itself is not faulty.
distractors.D: The sentence is unacceptable due to the redundancies.

questions.stem: Choose the sentence NEAREST in meaning to: 'According to the witness, he did not see the accused man near the scene of the crime.'

options.A: The witness testified that he did not see the accused man commit the crime.
options.B: The witness testified that the accused man was not seen by him near the place where the crime was committed.
options.C: The witness testified to seeing no evidence that the accused man had committed the crime.
options.D: The witness testified that the accused man was far from the location where the crime was committed.

questions.explanation: Option B preserves the meaning: the witness did not see the accused near the crime scene.

distractors.A: The original speaks about location, not the act of committing.
distractors.C: Original is about visibility, not about evidence.
distractors.D: Original says the witness did not see him, not that he was far away.

questions.stem: Choose the sentence NEAREST in meaning to: 'Cigarettes have been linked to lung cancer and, strangely enough, so has bacon.'

options.A: Cancer of the lungs links cigarettes, and, strangely enough, bacon.
options.B: It is rather peculiar that bacon, like cigarettes, is connected with lung cancer.
options.C: It is strange that bacon, quite unlike cigarettes, is linked to lung cancer.
options.D: It is no wonder at all that both cigarettes and bacon are linked to lung cancer.

questions.explanation: Option B preserves the meaning: surprisingly bacon, like cigarettes, has been linked to lung cancer.

distractors.A: Awkward wording that reverses the relationship.
distractors.C: Says 'unlike cigarettes', contradicting 'so has bacon' which means similarly.
distractors.D: Says 'no wonder at all', the opposite of the surprise in the original.

questions.stem: Select the ONE underlined part that is inappropriate, or D if the sentence is acceptable as it stands. (A) She was not feeling well at the beginning of the journey, (B) and sitting at the front of the car (C) made her feel worse. (D) No error.

options.A: She was not feeling well at the beginning of the journey
options.B: and sitting at the front of the car

questions.explanation: The sentence is grammatically and idiomatically correct; it has no error.

distractors.C: This clause uses correct verb form.

questions.stem: Select the ONE underlined part that is inappropriate, or D if acceptable. (A) Considering the chaos you would cause (B) if you were to resign, (C) I have no choice than to refuse your request. (D) No error.

options.A: Considering the chaos you would cause
options.C: I have no choice than to refuse your request

questions.explanation: The correct idiom is 'no choice but to', not 'no choice than to'.

distractors.D: The sentence contains an idiom error in part C.

questions.stem: Select the ONE underlined part that is inappropriate, or D if acceptable. (A) Tom, as well as the other persons, who need help (B) has promised to give his services (C) free of charge for any worthwhile cause. (D) No error.

options.A: Tom, as well as the other persons, who need help
options.B: has promised to give his services
options.C: free of charge for any worthwhile cause

questions.explanation: The relative clause is ambiguous; in this construction the singular antecedent 'Tom' requires 'who needs', or the placement of the clause and verb agreement is incorrect. Part A contains the issue.

distractors.C: This phrase is idiomatic and correct.
distractors.D: There is an error in part A.

questions.stem: Select the ONE underlined part that is inappropriate, or D if acceptable. (A) After embarrassing his colleague (B) his consceince bothered him (C) and he eventually apologized. (D) No error.
questions.explanation: 'Consceince' is misspelled; the correct spelling is 'conscience'.

distractors.A: This phrase is correctly spelled and constructed.
distractors.D: The sentence contains the spelling error in part B.

questions.stem: Select the ONE underlined part that is inappropriate, or D if acceptable. (A) The better of the two athletes (B) claim never to have used (C) the dangerous drugs found in his possession. (D) No error.

options.A: The better of the two athletes
options.C: the dangerous drugs found in his possession

questions.explanation: 'The better of the two athletes' is singular, so the verb should be 'claims', not 'claim'.

distractors.A: This phrase correctly uses 'better' (comparative for two).
distractors.D: The sentence contains a subject-verb agreement error in part B.

questions.stem: Choose the word NEAREST in meaning to the underlined word. It was impossible to <u>rectify</u> the error made by the computer since inaccurate information had been fed into it.
questions.explanation: 'Rectify' means to put right or correct, closest to 'fix'.

distractors.B: 'Pin' means to fasten, unrelated to correcting.
distractors.C: 'Aggravate' means to make worse, the opposite.
distractors.D: 'Determine' means to decide, not to correct.

questions.stem: Choose the word NEAREST in meaning to the underlined word. The flowers grow <u>profusely</u> in the garden.
questions.explanation: 'Profusely' means in great quantity or abundance, equivalent to 'plentifully'.

distractors.A: 'Wildly' suggests uncontrolled growth, not abundance per se.
distractors.B: 'Healthily' refers to good condition, not quantity.
distractors.D: 'Colourfully' describes appearance, not quantity.

questions.stem:
The phrase "Her garden looks" (line 1) is an example of

Her garden looks in through my window
Criss-crossed by the white lattice.
Coolers they call them but they are also
Hiding places for small girls playing.
Her garden stands neatly round her house
Travels politely unto the verandah
To sit in pots or hang
Leafily down from large, earth-coloured urns.

She lives with Mama, shepherding her with
her full body
The hesitant ins and outs of Mama's half-blind days.
Feeding her frail consciousness with edited
Gleaner news
And home-made chicken soup.

In her home, borrowed children touched her
china birds with hands
Wiped clean from eating sticky cakes, each
with a cherry on top.
Or press moist, breathless kisses round
The corners of her smile.

Sometimes she fills the space out
With music. Spreading out nostalgia through
Strings and flutes, old-fashioned love songs
Of blue moons and forever until.

Shameful peeping Tom, I sit silent in
My lattice watching the even shape of her days
To catch, just once, a wider open door behind
Her steady eyes.

But in her green-edged privacy, self-contained
She keeps the half-drawn shutters of her life
Open just so, and mocks my greed and restlessness
With a calm refusal to be other than she
seems.

Poem: "An Even Shape"
questions.explanation: Giving the garden the human ability to 'look' is personification.

Criss-crossed by the white lattice.
Coolers they call them but they are also
Hiding places for small girls playing.
Her garden stands neatly round her house
Travels politely unto the verandah
To sit in pots or hang
Leafily down from large, earth-coloured urns.

She lives with Mama, shepherding her with
her full body
The hesitant ins and outs of Mama's half-blind days.
Feeding her frail consciousness with edited
Gleaner news
And home-made chicken soup.

In her home, borrowed children touched her
china birds with hands
Wiped clean from eating sticky cakes, each
with a cherry on top.
Or press moist, breathless kisses round
The corners of her smile.

Sometimes she fills the space out
With music. Spreading out nostalgia through
Strings and flutes, old-fashioned love songs
Of blue moons and forever until.

Shameful peeping Tom, I sit silent in
My lattice watching the even shape of her days
To catch, just once, a wider open door behind
Her steady eyes.

But in her green-edged privacy, self-contained
She keeps the half-drawn shutters of her life
Open just so, and mocks my greed and restlessness
With a calm refusal to be other than she
seems.

Poem: "An Even Shape"

options.B: was a musician in an orchestra
options.C: spread all the musical instruments around the house
options.D: enjoyed listening to happy music which tells of love

questions.explanation: 'Old-fashioned love songs / Of blue moons and forever until' — she enjoys love songs.

questions.stem:
The mother's "thrust of the chin" (line 4) is done in

She had always been a small woman — short, light-boned, delicately structured — but now, under the white hospital sheet, she was becoming tiny. I thought of a doll with huge, fierce eyes. There had always been a fierceness in her. It showed in that angry, challenging thrust of the chin when she issued an opinion, and a great one she had always been for issuing opinions.

"I tell people exactly what's on my mind," she had been fond of boasting. "I tell them what I think, whether they like it or not." Often they had not liked it. She could be sarcastic to people in whom she detected evidence of the ignoramus or the fool.

"It's not always a good policy to tell people exactly what's on your mind," I used to caution her.

"If they don't like it, that's too bad," was her customary reply, "because that's the way I am."

And so she was. A formidable woman. Determined to speak her mind, determined to bend those who opposed her. In that time when I had known her best, my mother had hurled herself at life with chin thrust forward, eyes blazing, and an energy that made her seem always on the run.

She ran after squawking chickens, a knife in her hand, determined on the beheading that would put dinner in the pot. She ran when she made the beds, ran when she set the table. One Thanksgiving she burned herself badly when, running up from the cellar oven with the ceremonial turkey, she tripped on the stairs and tumbled back down ending on the bottom in the debris of giblets, hot gravy, and battered turkey. Life was combat, and victory was not for the lazy, the timid, the slugabed, the drugstore cowboy, the mushmouth afraid to tell people exactly what was on their mind whether people liked it or not. She ran.

But now her running was over.

Extract adapted from Russell Baker, "In My Day". Retrieved 23 February 2015 from http://www.douban.com/note/173685719.
questions.explanation: The 'angry, challenging thrust of the chin' marks defiance.

distractors.D: Contemplation is passive.

questions.stem:
The expression "given the green light to arcades" (line 4) means that

More than a quarter century ago, we were forced to wrestle with the thorny issue of casinos and the supposed ills and benefits which flow from them. In the end, a sensible solution emerged: allow some to remain while controlling their growth. Interestingly, we may argue about casinos, but we have given the green light to arcades where people gamble — day and night.

Some Caribbean governments have seemingly opened the door to yet another round of possible emotional debate with the decision to allow cruise ships lying at anchor to operate their casinos in their waters. Is this move a prelude to opening casinos on land?

Supporters of casino gambling have always said that casinos can and do function in a country without the moral decay that is routinely associated with them. Like most things, the pros and the cons about casino gambling are grossly exaggerated.

Those who favour them argue that casinos on land should be seen as another attraction that can be added to the country's tourism industry, if prudent controls are imposed. For example, The Bahamas, Antigua, Suriname and Sint Maarten are a few places where casino gambling is allowed and tourism authorities in those places have said that casino gambling has proven to be a stimulus to their tourism sector, without the moral decay that is routinely associated with them.

Opponents, on the other hand, insist that casinos lead to gambling addiction, crime, and worse, a debasement of society. With everything else that is happening in our islands — an upsurge of gangs, drugs and violent behaviour among our youth — that is the last thing that we may need right now. However, the case of the cruise ships keeping casinos open while docked on shore may cause little if any harm to the country and its people since few locals will have access to them.

Let's face it, Caribbean people gamble at bingo to raise money for churches; they flock to the tracks to watch horses run and bet millions annually on the outcome of the races. Additionally, many play the lotto daily and the English football pools weekly.

So if, as some are saying, this move of allowing gambling on cruise ships in port may be the first step to casinos on land, then we will not be totally surprised.

So let the debates begin.

Article: "Are Casinos Becoming a Reality?" Daily Nation, 26 April 2010, p. 8.

options.A: gambling is practised in the arcades
options.B: people gamble day and night under the light
options.C: arcades have been given permission to operate
options.D: green-coloured lights have been given to the arcades

questions.explanation: 'Given the green light' is an idiom for permission granted.

distractors.D: Literal misreading.

questions.stem:
The writer's use of the word "brain" (line 1) is meant to

This is an open request to try to find who is the 'brain' behind traffic management in Paolo. Does anybody know? I have tried Traffic Branch, not them; Highways, not them; Planning & Development, not them; in fact nobody wants to take responsibility for certain decisions and the mess that results.

For example, Mission Road was made one-way, north to south. Great idea! It worked, no traffic jams. In fact, many people were charged for going the wrong way up a one-way street. Then suddenly it's back to two-way and traffic for so! Same is true of Stoke Street. Somebody changed the 'Stop' sign at the corner for a few weeks, then they changed it back; nobody knows why.

But the best is Celestial Road in St John which is 'Wrecker Heaven'. Somebody made this road, which, apart from Broadway, is the widest street in Paolo, a 'no parking' street, either side, any time, and the 'Wrecker' is profiting from this.

There are four major office buildings on this road, all of which provide underground parking for staff, so some (usually the lower paid) have to park elsewhere and these are the persons whose cars the wrecker is taking away all day, everyday. Again the small man suffers!

Oh! there is an exception: if you have a Canadian High Commission sticker you can park and the wrecker will not touch your 'Diplomatic' car. Maybe we could all become Diplomats!

Then again, the two narrowest streets in Paolo allow parking; try Delino Street off Abby Lane, you can hardly pass!

Who is the 'brain' behind this mess? It seems the more cars we put on the road, the less parking somebody wants to allow. Who is this brain? No doubt a small man with a BIG, BIG car who has no name!

Letter on traffic management in "Paolo"

options.B: make fun of the poor decisions

questions.explanation: 'Brain' is sarcastic — the writer mocks the decisions.

distractors.D: Mocks, not compliments.

• Flying 40 000 miles qualifies members for a Gold Membership, while flying 80 000 miles qualifies members for an Executive Gold Membership.

options.D: become members of the Miles programme

questions.explanation: The heading itself says 'JOIN OUR CARIBBEAN MILES PROGRAMME'.

questions.stem: Their actions showed that they were firm in their rejection of the new law. (Choose the word closest to OPPOSITE in meaning to the underlined word 'rejection'.)
questions.explanation: The opposite of 'rejection' (refusing to accept) is 'acceptance' (agreeing to receive or take).

distractors.A: Ignorance means a lack of knowledge, not the opposite of rejection.
distractors.C: Application means putting something into use, unrelated to acceptance or rejection.
distractors.D: Understanding means comprehension, not the opposite of refusing.

questions.stem: Every effort was made to ensure his arrival. (Choose the word closest to OPPOSITE in meaning to the underlined word 'ensure'.)
questions.explanation: 'Ensure' means to make certain. 'Jeopardize' means to put at risk or endanger, which is the opposite of making certain.

distractors.A: Avoid means to keep away from, which is not the direct opposite of ensure.
distractors.B: Predict means to foretell, unrelated to ensuring.
distractors.C: Analyse means to examine in detail, not the opposite of ensure.

questions.stem: Paul was enthusiastic about attending the final game. (Choose the word closest to OPPOSITE in meaning to the underlined word 'enthusiastic'.)
questions.explanation: 'Enthusiastic' means showing intense and eager enjoyment. 'Unconcerned' means showing a lack of interest, the opposite.

distractors.A: Eager is a synonym, not an antonym of enthusiastic.
distractors.B: Anxious suggests worry, not the opposite of enthusiastic.
distractors.C: Curious means interested in learning, closer to enthusiastic than its opposite.

questions.stem: The compassionate man was known by many in the crowd. (Choose the word closest to OPPOSITE in meaning to the underlined word 'compassionate'.)
questions.explanation: 'Compassionate' means showing sympathy and concern. 'Uncaring' is the direct opposite.

distractors.A: Fussy means hard to please, unrelated to compassion.
distractors.B: Proud relates to self-esteem, not the opposite of compassion.
distractors.D: Dissatisfied means displeased, unrelated to caring for others.

questions.stem: As a result of the available evidence, the accused was apprehended and no further action was taken. (Choose the word closest to OPPOSITE in meaning to 'apprehended'.)
questions.explanation: 'Apprehended' means arrested or caught. 'Released' means set free, which is the opposite.

distractors.A: Evicted means forced out of a property, not the opposite of apprehended.
distractors.C: Cautioned means warned, which still implies being held by authority.
distractors.D: Summoned means called to appear, not the opposite of being apprehended.

questions.stem: Select the option that BEST describes the sentence: 'He quickly reversed back the car into the street to avoid being hit by the oncoming vehicle.'

options.A: The sentence is too wordy, that is, repetitive or contains redundancies.
options.B: The sentence contains cliches or misused metaphors.
options.C: The sentence is incorrect grammatically or faulty in diction.
options.D: The sentence is acceptable as it stands.

questions.explanation: 'Reversed back' is redundant because to reverse already means to move backward.

distractors.B: There are no cliches or misused metaphors in this sentence.
distractors.C: The sentence is grammatically correct; the issue is redundancy.
distractors.D: The sentence has a redundancy and is not acceptable as it stands.

questions.stem: Select the option that BEST describes the sentence: 'It's not absolutely necessary for it to be done today.'

options.A: The sentence is too wordy, that is, repetitive or contains redundancies.
options.B: The sentence contains cliches or misused metaphors.
options.C: The sentence is incorrect grammatically or faulty in diction.
options.D: The sentence is acceptable as it stands.

questions.explanation: 'Absolutely necessary' is redundant. 'Necessary' on its own already conveys the meaning.

distractors.B: There are no cliches or misused metaphors in this sentence.
distractors.C: The sentence is grammatically correct; the issue is wordiness.
distractors.D: The sentence contains a redundancy and is not acceptable as it stands.

questions.stem: Select the option that BEST describes the sentence: 'We must ensure that we do not put the cart before the horse in this matter.'

options.A: The sentence is too wordy, that is, repetitive or contains redundancies.
options.B: The sentence contains cliches or misused metaphors.
options.C: The sentence is incorrect grammatically or faulty in diction.
options.D: The sentence is acceptable as it stands.

questions.explanation: 'Put the cart before the horse' is a well-known cliche meaning to do things in the wrong order.

distractors.A: The sentence is not wordy or redundant.
distractors.D: The use of a cliche makes the sentence less acceptable as it stands.

questions.stem: Choose the misspelled word, or 'No error' if all are correct: 'The (A) principal bought the school (B) furniture from that (C) store. (D) No error'
questions.explanation: All three underlined words are correctly spelled.

questions.stem: Choose the misspelled word, or 'No error' if all are correct: 'The (A) minister complained that the (B) number of persons selected to perform was (C) inadeqaute. (D) No error'
questions.explanation: 'Inadeqaute' is misspelled. The correct spelling is 'inadequate'.

distractors.D: There is an error, so 'No error' is incorrect.

questions.stem: Choose the misspelled word, or 'No error' if all are correct: 'The (A) reciept of his letter (B) assured me that such an (C) incident would not be tolerated. (D) No error'
questions.explanation: 'Reciept' is misspelled. The correct spelling is 'receipt' (i before e except after c).

distractors.D: There is an error, so 'No error' is incorrect.

questions.stem: Choose the misspelled word, or 'No error' if all are correct: 'His (A) colleague thought that the (B) accommodation provided was (C) substandard. (D) No error'
questions.explanation: All three underlined words are correctly spelled.

distractors.B: 'Accommodation' is correctly spelled with two c's and two m's.

questions.stem: Choose the misspelled word, or 'No error' if all are correct: 'It is my (A) privilege to give you some (B) advise concerning proper (C) nutrition. (D) No error'
questions.explanation: 'Advise' is misused here. As a noun meaning guidance, the correct word is 'advice'. 'Advise' is the verb form.

distractors.D: There is an error in the use of 'advise' instead of 'advice'.

questions.stem: Revise the sentence: '"It's going to be alright," Mum assured me.' Begin with: 'Mum assured me that ___'
questions.explanation: Reported speech shifts the present to the past. 'It's going to be alright' becomes 'it was going to be alright'.

distractors.A: 'There' does not appropriately fit the reported speech construction.
distractors.C: 'Simply' changes the meaning and does not preserve the original sentence.
distractors.D: 'Only just' alters the meaning of the original.

questions.stem: Revise the sentence: 'In your travels you will meet people of different cultures.' Begin with: 'Your travels ___'
questions.explanation: 'Your travels expose you to people of different cultures' preserves the original meaning - travelling brings you into contact with diverse cultures.

distractors.B: 'Submit you to' suggests forced compliance, altering the meaning.
distractors.C: 'Burdened with' implies a negative experience, changing the meaning.
distractors.D: 'Be invited along with' does not preserve the original meaning.

questions.stem: Revise the sentence: 'On learning of the impending hurricane, many persons rushed to the supermarket.' Begin with: 'Many persons rushed ___'
questions.explanation: 'Many persons rushed to the supermarket when they learnt of the impending hurricane' preserves the temporal relationship.

distractors.A: 'So they learnt' reverses cause and effect.
distractors.B: 'But they learned' creates an unintended contrast.
distractors.D: 'For they were learning' is grammatically awkward and changes meaning.

questions.stem: Revise the sentence: 'The cricket match ended abruptly when many of the spectators stormed the field.' Begin with: 'Many of the spectators stormed ___'
questions.explanation: 'Many of the spectators stormed the field, so the cricket match ended abruptly' preserves the cause-effect relationship.

distractors.B: 'But' creates a contrast, not a cause-effect relationship.
distractors.C: 'Getting' does not fit the meaning grammatically.
distractors.D: 'Preventing' changes the meaning - spectators caused the match to end.

questions.stem: Revise the sentence: 'During my time at school, I was involved in extracurricular activities.' Begin with: 'I was involved in ___'
questions.explanation: 'I was involved in extracurricular activities while I was at school' preserves the temporal relationship of the original sentence.

distractors.C: 'For my stay' does not appropriately preserve the meaning.
distractors.D: 'When staying' is grammatically awkward in this construction.

questions.stem: It was impossible to rectify the error made by the computer since inaccurate information had been fed into it. (Choose the word NEAREST IN MEANING to 'rectify'.)
questions.explanation: 'Rectify' means to put right or correct something. 'Correct' is the closest synonym.

distractors.A: 'Alter' means to change, but not necessarily to fix.
distractors.B: 'Erase' means to remove entirely, not to put right.
distractors.D: 'Determine' means to decide or find out, unrelated to fixing.

questions.stem: The flowers grow profusely in the garden. (Choose the word NEAREST IN MEANING to 'profusely'.)
questions.explanation: 'Profusely' means in great quantity or abundance. 'Abundantly' is the closest synonym.

distractors.A: 'Wildly' suggests uncontrolled growth, not necessarily abundance.
distractors.B: 'Healthily' refers to good health, not abundance.
distractors.D: 'Colourfully' relates to colour, not quantity.

questions.stem: For the first time in a decade, the company disclosed a deficit in its account. (Choose the word NEAREST IN MEANING to 'disclosed'.)
questions.explanation: 'Disclosed' means to make known publicly. 'Revealed' is the closest synonym.

distractors.A: 'Altered' means to change, not to make known.
distractors.C: 'Fabricated' means to invent or make up.
distractors.D: 'Concealed' means to hide, the opposite of disclosed.

questions.stem: Bush fires are prevalent during the dry season. (Choose the word NEAREST IN MEANING to 'prevalent'.)
questions.explanation: 'Prevalent' means widespread or common in a particular area or time. 'Widespread' is the closest synonym.

distractors.A: 'Practised' means done frequently as a habit, not the same as widespread.
distractors.B: 'Curtailed' means reduced, the opposite of prevalent.
distractors.D: 'Threatening' describes danger, not how common something is.

questions.stem: During the final stages of the trial, a new witness was called to corroborate the testimony of the accused. (Choose the word NEAREST IN MEANING to 'corroborate'.)
questions.explanation: 'Corroborate' means to confirm or give support to a statement. 'Support' is the closest synonym.

distractors.A: 'Clarify' means to make clear, not to confirm.
distractors.C: 'Conclude' means to bring to an end.
distractors.D: 'Compliment' means to praise, unrelated to confirming.

questions.stem:
There is a "single kite" (line 1) left in the sky MOST likely because

When a single kite is left
in evening sky
and I stand
black against its saffron
pulling in the night-covers
with a single thread
I think of you.

When chickens search the warmth
of a hen's wings;
When bats break a calm lake
like scissors on a warm fabric
where a lone fisherman waits
alone, on the hill,
I think of you.

Poem: "Taste of Endless Fruit"

options.C: the cover of night has descended
options.D: the other kite flyers are asleep

questions.explanation: The evening sky is being 'pulled in' for the night — it is late.

distractors.C: Night covers still being pulled IN.
distractors.D: Not stated.

questions.stem:
At what time of day did the MAIN events of the passage occur?

The truck appeared as soon as the sun set, bursting into the plaza from some street through which the nocturnal wave of murkiness and stars advanced. At once it proceeded to circle the tree-lined rectangle a number of times — secret, stealthy — like an animal searching for somewhere to rest. The old men sitting in a row on the long half-moon shaped concrete bench turned their heads in time to see it make its slow entrance. It passed in front of them (its dark blue brimming with shoals of luminescent decal art) and, turning off the headlights, stopped to one side of an arbour blown over a short while before by the hurricane. It was the same enormous, outlandish truck that had gone through the pueblo that afternoon, deafening everyone with its loudspeaker: it would have been easy to follow it by ear through the maze of streets.

It was a navy blue truck, covered with decals of fish, that ended by circling around and around the plaza, from where the echo reverberated, moving away through the series of side streets that fed onto it. Then the truck had gone into one of them and disappeared, creating an unusual momentary silence everywhere, as if it had robbed the entire village of sound. Now it was here again, and a man got out, who seemed with his glance to take in the atmosphere of the public park and the white heights of the temple, dotted with already sleeping doves.

He moved towards the back part of the vehicle and struggled with the doors for a while. From inside he brought out several bolts of a greyish material that he unrolled on the ground. In about an hour the bolts proved to be canvas, forming a small tent attached to the truck. The entrance to this sort of country house was covered with an arch of multi-coloured light bulbs that, once turned on, outlined three shining words: THE BEAUTIFUL MELUSINE, in the black air of the night. At once the man went inside, like a mollusc into the shell, and he didn't show himself again until the town clock struck eight. By then the line of spectators already extended several times around the square of the plaza.

Extract from Edgardo Sanabria Santaliz, "After the Hurricane", New England Review and Bread Loaf Quarterly, Vol. 7, No. 4.
questions.explanation: The truck 'appeared as soon as the sun set' — sunset is evening; the staging takes place in the evening/early-night.

distractors.D: Not morning.

It passed in front of them (its dark blue brimming with shoals of luminescent decal art) and, turning off the headlights, stopped to one side of an arbour blown over a short while before by the hurricane. It was the same enormous, outlandish truck that had gone through the pueblo that afternoon, deafening everyone with its loudspeaker: it would have been easy to follow it by ear through the maze of streets.

It was a navy blue truck, covered with decals of fish, that ended by circling around and around the plaza, from where the echo reverberated, moving away through the series of side streets that fed onto it. Then the truck had gone into one of them and disappeared, creating an unusual momentary silence everywhere, as if it had robbed the entire village of sound. Now it was here again, and a man got out, who seemed with his glance to take in the atmosphere of the public park and the white heights of the temple, dotted with already sleeping doves.

He moved towards the back part of the vehicle and struggled with the doors for a while. From inside he brought out several bolts of a greyish material that he unrolled on the ground. In about an hour the bolts proved to be canvas, forming a small tent attached to the truck. The entrance to this sort of country house was covered with an arch of multi-coloured light bulbs that, once turned on, outlined three shining words: THE BEAUTIFUL MELUSINE, in the black air of the night. At once the man went inside, like a mollusc into the shell, and he didn't show himself again until the town clock struck eight. By then the line of spectators already extended several times around the square of the plaza.

Extract from Edgardo Sanabria Santaliz, "After the Hurricane", New England Review and Bread Loaf Quarterly, Vol. 7, No. 4.

options.C: lack of entertainment in the village
options.D: eagerness of the villagers for entertainment

questions.explanation: 'The line of spectators already extended several times around the square' — the queue is enormous, showing how eager the villagers are.

distractors.C: Lack is implied but eagerness is direct.

questions.stem:
According to paragraph 1 (lines 1-4), deaths from heart disease, cancer and debility are on the increase because of

Industrialization has created a new set of intangible threats to health. These threats have presented themselves in socially accepted forms: the socially acquired glamour of cigarette smoking, the pleasures of alcohol and the spurious release gained through drugs, which have resulted in many cases of heart disease, cancer and debility.

Since many developing countries are succumbing to the same habits and dependence, they lay themselves open to the same diseases. One can work by one rough rule-of-thumb, says Dr H. Hambuchen, Chief Statistician of WHO's Global Epidemiological Surveillance and Health Situation Assessment: once a country achieves a life expectancy at birth of 60 years, cancer rapidly moves up in the ranking of health problems and causes of death.

We now need to work hard and determinedly to implement decisions on lifestyle change. These will depend on two aspects: the personal choices made and the choices that are only possible if national policies and health strategies break new ground.

Nothing illustrates these aspects more succinctly than smoking. About a quarter of all cigarette smokers are killed before their time by tobacco. Most would have lived on for another 5 to 10 years, the average being an extra 10 to 15 years. If they stopped smoking before they developed cancer or heart or lung disease, they could have averted the risk of dying from tobacco.

Government policies can strengthen individual choice and prevent the growth of the smoking habit. Restrictions which lower already overstretched tax revenues may be collecting taxes and buying jobs in the painting of tobacco and the manufacture of cigarettes, but this is the cost of expensive, long-term medical care, and in human suffering and premature death.

Some causes of ill health can be removed by political action and financial investment. But some, bound about by the way people live, can be removed by people deciding to change their way of living.

Source unknown.

options.A: the dangers posed by modern living
options.C: growing substance abuse in today's society
options.D: longer life expectancy in recent years

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 attributes the increase in heart disease, cancer and debility specifically to cigarette smoking, alcohol and drugs — i.e. growing substance abuse in today's society.

distractors.A: Plausible reading but the passage does not support it — see explanation.
distractors.B: Plausible reading but the passage does not support it — see explanation.
distractors.D: Plausible reading but the passage does not support it — see explanation.

questions.stem:
The writer's use of the word "brain" (line 1) is meant to

This is an open request to try to find who is the 'brain' behind traffic management in Paolo. Does anybody know? I have tried Traffic Branch, not them; Highways, not them; Planning & Development, not them; in fact nobody wants to take responsibility for certain decisions and the mess that results.

For example, Mission Road was made one-way, north to south. Great idea! It worked, no traffic jams. In fact, many people were charged for going the wrong way up a one-way street. Then suddenly it's back to two-way and traffic for so! Same is true of Stoke Street. Somebody changed the 'Stop' sign at the corner for a few weeks, then they changed it back; nobody knows why.

But the best is Celestial Road in St John which is 'Wrecker Heaven'. Somebody made this road, which, apart from Broadway, is the widest street in Paolo, a 'no parking' street, either side, any time, and the 'Wrecker' is profiting from this.

There are four major office buildings on this road, all of which provide underground parking for staff, so some (usually the lower paid) have to park elsewhere and these are the persons whose cars the wrecker is taking away all day, everyday. Again the small man suffers!

Oh! there is an exception: if you have a Canadian High Commission sticker you can park and the wrecker will not touch your 'Diplomatic' car. Maybe we could all become Diplomats!

Then again, the two narrowest streets in Paolo allow parking; try Delino Street off Abby Lane, you can hardly pass!

Who is the 'brain' behind this mess? It seems the more cars we put on the road, the less parking somebody wants to allow. Who is this brain? No doubt a small man with a BIG, BIG car who has no name!

Letter on traffic management in "Paolo"
questions.explanation: 'Brain' is sarcastic — the writer mocks the decision-makers.

distractors.D: Mocks, not compliments.
